wines.name: Chateau Angelus
wines.classification: Saint-Emilion Grand Cru (withdrew from Premier Grand Cru Classe A from 2022 vintage; certified organic 2021)
wines.oak_regime: 18-24 months in 100% new French oak; 50% of Cabernet Franc portion aged in neutral foudres from recent vintages for freshness

taste.summary: Merlot and Cabernet Franc from 27 classified hectares at the foot of Saint-Emilion's slope. Generous, opulent and structured, with dark cherry, violets and cedar complexity; certified organic from the 2021 vintage.

pairings.dish: Roast beef ribeye with bordelaise sauce
pairings.why: The wine's dark-fruit density and firm tannin is the textbook match for marbled beef with red-wine reduction.
Cherry fruit in the glaze mirrors the wine's dark cherry core; duck fat integrates the velvety tannins.
Smoky vegetable notes find a peer in the wine's cedar and spice; lamb's gaminess is softened by ripe Merlot.
Earthy black truffle echoes cedar and graphite; the wine's body supports the dish's richness.

history.summary: Chateau Angelus traces its origins to a 16th-century estate, though the modern high-quality era begins under Hubert de Bouard de Laforest, who took charge in 1985. The estate was classified as Grand Cru Classe B in 1969 and promoted to Premier Grand Cru Classe A in 2012. Organic farming began in 2018, with Ecocert certification obtained in 2021. In 2021 the estate announced its withdrawal from all future Saint-Emilion classifications, effective from the 2022 vintage. Stephanie de Bouard-Rivoal is co-owner and CEO.

milestones.event: First classification as Premier Grand Cru Classe B
Hubert de Bouard assumes management; transformation of quality begins
Promoted to Premier Grand Cru Classe A in the revised classification
Conversion to organic farming across all 42 hectares
Ecocert organic certification obtained; withdrawal from classification announced

wines.description: Grand vin from Chateau Angelus, certified organic since 2021. Opulent Merlot and Cabernet Franc blend from 27 classified hectares at the foot of Saint-Emilion's limestone slope; full-bodied with velvety dark-fruit character.

wines.name: Hommage a Elisabeth Bouchet
wines.oak_regime: Aged primarily in neutral foudres (80-100%) with minimal new oak; approach varies by vintage

taste.summary: 100% Cabernet Franc from two old-vine parcels aged 65-100 years at the pied de cote. Produced in tiny quantities (5-9 barriques) only in exceptional vintages, this is a monument to the iron-mineral potential of old Cabernet Franc on Saint-Emilion limestone.

pairings.dish: Venison loin with black pepper jus
pairings.why: Game intensity meets the wine's iron-mineral structure; the aromatic density requires a dish of equal weight.
Raw beef and truffle earthy complexity echo the wine's graphite and dark-violet registers.

pairings.dish: Aged Basque sheep's cheese with quince
pairings.why: Iron-mineral tannin is softened by the cheese's crystalline texture; quince sweetness provides counterpoint.

history.summary: Created to honor Elisabeth Bouchet, great-grandmother of co-owner Stephanie de Bouard-Rivoal. The Bouchet surname is the historical Right Bank name for Cabernet Franc, making the wine's 100% varietal composition a direct tribute. Experimental bottlings were made from 2003 but the 2016 vintage was the first commercial release. Produced only in the greatest vintages from five barrels of the estate's oldest Cabernet Franc vines.

milestones.event: Experimental micro-bottlings of old-vine Cabernet Franc begin
First commercial release of Hommage a Elisabeth Bouchet

wines.description: 100% old-vine Cabernet Franc micro-cuvee from Chateau Angelus, first commercially released in 2016. Produced in 5-9 barriques only in exceptional vintages from vines aged 65-100 years; a concentrated, mineral monument to the pied de cote limestone.

wines.name: Le Carillon de l'Angelus
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in a mix of new and second-use French oak

taste.summary: The second wine of Angelus, Merlot-dominant with a forward, polished character. Bright red fruit, soft spice and floral tones make it approachable younger than the grand vin while retaining the estate's Merlot generosity.

pairings.why: Medium-weight fruit and restrained oak work with mild poultry; herbal tarragon echoes the floral register.
Polished tannins complement lean pork; bright acidity lifts the dish.
Creamy Brie integrates with the wine's gentle tannin; fruit enhances mild dairy notes.

history.summary: Le Carillon de l'Angelus was first produced in the 1987 vintage, one of the earliest second wines created on the Right Bank. Named after the estate's bell-tower motif, it draws on a variable blend of parcels to deliver an accessible, forward style. The wine has evolved alongside the grand vin and reflects high production standards throughout.

milestones.event: First vintage of Le Carillon de l'Angelus produced

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Angelus, first produced in 1987. Merlot-dominant with a forward, approachable profile of polished red fruit and soft spice; from the same organically farmed estate as the grand vin.

wines.name: Chapelle d'Ausone
wines.oak_regime: 20 months in French oak barrels; same conditions as grand vin but from youngest vines

taste.summary: The second wine of Ausone, sourced from the youngest Cabernet Franc and Merlot vines on the same limestone hillside. Crisp, elegant and unmistakably mineral, with a fruity register that opens earlier than the grand vin.

pairings.why: The wine's restrained fruit and high acidity complement mild poultry; earthy mushroom mirrors the mineral notes.

pairings.dish: Lamb chops with herbs de Provence
pairings.why: Herbal aromatics in the wine echo the herb crust; firm tannin integrates with lamb fat.

pairings.dish: Charcuterie board with pate de campagne
pairings.why: Bright acidity cuts through pork fat; red-fruit register pairs with classic Bordeaux charcuterie.

history.summary: Chapelle d'Ausone was created in 1995 as the second wine of Chateau Ausone, drawing on the youngest vines of the 7-hectare estate. Named after the chapel that overlooks the vineyard, it accounts for 10-20% of estate production depending on vintage. Alain Vauthier was among the first Right Bank producers to impose strict quality-driven selection for second-wine production.

milestones.event: First vintage of Chapelle d'Ausone produced
Re-released aged library vintages (2011, 2012, 2016) alongside current releases

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Ausone from the same 7-hectare limestone hillside, first produced in 1995. Mineral, elegant and Cabernet Franc-expressive, it opens earlier than the grand vin while retaining Ausone's signature stony precision.

wines.name: Chateau Ausone
wines.classification: Saint-Emilion Grand Cru (withdrew from Premier Grand Cru Classe A from 2022 vintage)
wines.oak_regime: Up to 24 months in 100% new French oak barrels; cold pre-fermentation maceration; 54-hectolitre oak vat fermentation

taste.summary: From just 7 hectares of steep limestone slopes, Ausone is a wine of concentration, precision and mineral tension. Cabernet Franc dominates, giving iron-rich structure, dried-flower fragrance and an extraordinary seamless finish.

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with black truffle jus
pairings.why: Iron-mineral tannin structure integrates with lanolin and truffle earthiness; Cabernet Franc lifts the finish.
Slow-cooked collagen softens the wine's firm structure; the rich sauce mirrors its dense dark fruit.
Game intensity pairs with the wine's iron and dried-herb register; acidity cuts through the fat.
Mineral salt and nuttiness in aged sheep's cheese echo Ausone's limestone terroir character.

history.summary: Named for the 4th-century Gallo-Roman poet Ausonius, the Ausone estate traces its viticultural history to ancient Roman terraces cut into the limestone hillside of Saint-Emilion. The modern era begins with Alain Vauthier taking full control in 1997 after a prolonged family dispute. Under his direction, and with daughter Pauline as technical director since 2005, Ausone rose to become a benchmark of micro-production precision. The estate declined participation in the 2022 Saint-Emilion reclassification, withdrawing alongside Cheval Blanc and Angelus.

milestones.event: First modern commercial vintages recorded
Alain Vauthier assumes full control; quality transformation begins
Pauline Vauthier joins as oenologist, now technical director
Estate announces withdrawal from all future Saint-Emilion classifications

wines.description: Micro-production grand vin from 7 hectares of ancient limestone hillside in Saint-Emilion, managed by the Vauthier family. One of Bordeaux's most mineral, long-lived wines; Cabernet Franc-dominant with iron-rich precision.

wines.name: Chateau Bauduc Bordeaux Superieur
wines.oak_regime: Aged in tank and barrel; accessible, fruit-forward style

taste.summary: Bauduc's entry-level claret; a straightforward Merlot-Cabernet blend offering reliable, juicy red-fruit character and soft tannins at an accessible price. Drink now or hold for up to seven years. The wine is consistently good for its level.

pairings.why: Soft tannins and ripe red fruit are a weeknight-friendly match for simple roast poultry.
Juicy, fruit-forward style holds up to burger intensity; soft tannins do not clash with the cheese.
Classic Italian-French hybrid pairing; the wine's plum and soft tannins complement the tomato-meat ragu.

history.summary: The estate red of Chateau Bauduc has been produced since the Quinney family's first vintage in 2000. It is bottled under the Bordeaux Superieur AOC, giving it slightly more structure than basic Bordeaux. It is the most widely available Bauduc wine and functions as the estate's introduction wine for new customers.

milestones.event: First Bordeaux Superieur red produced by the Quinney family at Bauduc

wines.description: Bauduc's entry-level claret; Merlot-Cabernet Bordeaux Superieur, soft, juicy, and fruit-forward. Reliable everyday drinking from the English-owned Entre-Deux-Mers estate at accessible prices.

wines.name: Bauduc Les Trois Hectares Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Partial barrel fermentation; aged on lees for additional texture

taste.summary: Single-plot Semillon from 75-year-old vines in a three-hectare block; the house premium white. Dry and elegant with the waxy, textured Semillon character that recalls white Pessac-Leognan at a fraction of the price. Anne Krebiehl MW-praised.

pairings.why: Semillon texture and melon fruit match the sweet, caramelised crust of roasted scallops; cauliflower cream echoes the lanolin note.
Rich shellfish and butter demand a textured white; Semillon's body and lime acidity provide the ideal counterpoint.

pairings.dish: Chicken and leek pie with shortcrust pastry
pairings.why: The gentle oak and melon notes complement creamy chicken filling; Sauvignon Blanc freshness cuts the pastry richness.

history.summary: Les Trois Hectares Blanc was created to showcase the Quinney family's single plot of 75-year-old Semillon vines, a rarity in Entre-Deux-Mers. The Semillon-dominant blend produces a dry, elegant white with longevity that challenges the idea that Bordeaux Blanc needs to be Sauvignon-led.

milestones.event: First vintage of Les Trois Hectares Blanc from old-vine Semillon plot

wines.description: Single-plot Semillon (85%) from 75-year-old vines; dry, textured, and elegant. Anne Krebiehl MW praised for lime pith and melon skin. Limited production; showcases old-vine Entre-Deux-Mers Semillon character.

wines.name: Bauduc Les Trois Hectares Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Aged in small French oak barrels; precise new oak percentage not disclosed

taste.summary: Bauduc's premium red from a single Bordeaux Superieur block: 75% Merlot, 25% Cabernet Sauvignon aged in small barrels for added structure. Anne Krebiehl MW describes it as juicy, plush, and beautifully open with oak-kissed fruit.

pairings.dish: Roast pork loin with apple sauce
pairings.why: Medium body and ripe cherry fruit complement mild pork; the gentle oak spice adds savour.
Classic Bordeaux-and-steak pairing at an accessible price; Cabernet Sauvignon structure holds up to the beef.
Plush Merlot fruit and soft tannins work perfectly with rich duck fat; vanilla oak bridges the toast.

history.summary: Les Trois Hectares Rouge was introduced alongside the white from the same block with the Quinney family's first vintages at Bauduc in 2000. The Bordeaux Superieur classification (higher minimum ageing requirements than basic Bordeaux AOC) reflects the wine's greater ambition and structure. It has become the estate's best-known red.

milestones.event: First vintage of Les Trois Hectares Rouge by the Quinney family
Wine praised by Decanter for smoked and toasted fruit with sinewy balance

wines.description: Bauduc's flagship Bordeaux Superieur red; 75% Merlot, 25% Cabernet Sauvignon from a single block, aged in small French oak barrels. Juicy and plush with oak-kissed fruit; Anne Krebiehl MW and Tom Gilbey praised.

wines.name: Chateau Bauduc Sauvignon Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel fermented and aged on lees; no oak contact

taste.summary: Bauduc's benchmark white and most popular wine: 100% Sauvignon Blanc, stainless steel fermented for maximum freshness and varietal definition. Crisp, dry, and food-versatile; house white at Rick Stein's fish restaurants for over a decade.

pairings.dish: Grilled sea bream with herb butter
pairings.why: Crisp Sauvignon acidity lifts white fish; citrus and grass notes complement herb butter.
The racy acidity and mineral freshness are a classic pairing with briny Atlantic oysters.
Sauvignon Blanc's grassy note mirrors asparagus; the wine's acidity cuts through the richness of hollandaise.
High acidity and herbaceous character are a textbook match for fresh goats cheese.

history.summary: Gavin and Angela Quinney purchased Chateau Bauduc in 1999 and made their first vintage in 2000, with the Sauvignon Blanc quickly becoming the estate's signature wine. The wine's consistent quality earned it a long-running placement at Rick Stein's seafood restaurants, giving it a profile unusual for a Bordeaux Blanc at this price level.

milestones.event: First vintage produced by Gavin and Angela Quinney
Wine placed as house white at Rick Stein's seafood restaurants

wines.description: Bauduc's flagship 100% Sauvignon Blanc; stainless steel fermented on lees for maximum freshness. House white at Rick Stein's for over a decade; crisp, dry, and versatile at accessible Bordeaux Blanc prices.

wines.name: Chateau Beau-Sejour Becot
wines.classification: Saint-Emilion Premier Grand Cru Classe B (2022 classification)
wines.oak_regime: Aged in 50-65% new French oak; approximately 20-25% of harvest in clay amphora and foudres from recent vintages

taste.finish: long, silk and velvet texture with dark-fruit persistence
taste.summary: From 16.5 hectares of ancient underground cellars carved into the limestone of Saint-Emilion's plateau, Beau-Sejour Becot offers silk and velvet textures with floral aromatics, dark fruits and a crushed-stone mineral lift. Recent vintages show increasing use of amphora for freshness.

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with tapenade crust
pairings.why: The wine's dark-fruit density and firm tannin structure integrates with lamb richness; olive tapenade echoes its Mediterranean herbal registers.
Earthy cep mushroom and silky beef align with the wine's graphite-mineral backbone and dark-cherry fruit.
Plum reduction mirrors the wine's blueberry and cassis palate; duck fat softens the velvety tannins.
Black truffle's earthy depth amplifies the crushed-stone minerality characteristic of the plateau limestone.

history.summary: Chateau Beau-Sejour Becot is a 16.5-hectare estate on the limestone plateau of Saint-Emilion, run by the sixth-generation Becot family. The estate is notable for its ancient troglodytic wine cellar tunnels carved into the limestone. Classified as Premier Grand Cru Classe B in 1969, it was temporarily demoted in 1985 following the unauthorized expansion of the vineyard, but was restored to Premier Grand Cru Classe B in 1996. Juliette Becot and her husband Julien Barthe now lead the estate, continuing the family tradition of quality.

milestones.event: Classified as Premier Grand Cru Classe B
Temporarily demoted following unauthorized vineyard expansion
Restored to Premier Grand Cru Classe B classification
Status as Premier Grand Cru Classe B confirmed in latest classification

wines.description: Premier Grand Cru Classe B from the Becot family's 16.5-hectare limestone-plateau estate in Saint-Emilion. Silk and velvet textures with floral dark-fruit aromatics and crushed-stone minerality; notable for ancient troglodytic cellars and increasing use of amphora in production.

verified.address_quoted: Lieu dit La Carte, 33330 Saint-Emilion, France

wines.name: Petit Becot
wines.oak_regime: Aged in used French oak barrels; lower new-oak ratio than grand vin

taste.finish: medium and approachable with fresh fruit
taste.summary: The renamed second wine of Beau-Sejour Becot (formerly Tournelle de Beau-Sejour Becot), renamed Petit Becot in 2012. Merlot-dominant with fresh red cherry character and gentle tannin; designed for early drinking pleasure.

pairings.why: Juicy red fruit and medium tannin complement the burger's beef patty; mild cheese integrates easily.
Red fruit register echoes the apple; gentle acidity cuts through pork fat.
Delicate poultry terrine matches the wine's fresh, medium-bodied profile; herb accompaniment finds the wine's light herbal notes.

history.summary: Petit Becot is the renamed second wine of Chateau Beau-Sejour Becot. Previously known as Tournelle de Beau-Sejour Becot, the name was modernized to Petit Becot in 2012. Produced from younger vines and parcels not selected for the grand vin, it reflects the same plateau limestone terroir in a more accessible register.

milestones.event: Renamed Petit Becot (previously Tournelle de Beau-Sejour Becot)

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Beau-Sejour Becot, renamed Petit Becot in 2012 (previously Tournelle de Beau-Sejour Becot). Merlot-dominant, fresh and approachable with red cherry character from the same limestone plateau terroir.

verified.address_quoted: Lieu dit La Carte, 33330 Saint-Emilion, France

wines.name: Château de Camensac
wines.oak_regime: 35-70% new French oak barrels, 17-20 months

taste.summary: A revived Fifth Growth finding its stride under the Merlaut family: Cabernet-forward Haut-Médoc structure with dark fruit, cedar and characteristic menthol freshness from deep gravel soils near Saint-Julien.

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with rosemary and garlic
pairings.why: The wine's cedar and dark fruit structure complement the herb-forward minerality of roasted rack of lamb.
The wine's firm structure and dark fruit gain complexity from slow-cooked beef with earthy root vegetables.
Earthy truffle and mushroom umami resonate with the wine's truffle-inflected nose and dark fruit.

history.summary: Château Camensac was classified in 1855 but fell into decline. The Forner family of La Rioja bought it in 1964 and revived it with the advice of Emile Peynaud. In 2005, the Merlaut-Villars family took over and launched a substantial replanting and winery modernisation programme, pushing quality steadily upward.

milestones.event: Classified as Cinquième Cru Classé in the Bordeaux 1855 Classification
Forner family acquires and revives the estate with Emile Peynaud's advice
Merlaut-Villars family purchase and begin extensive replanting and modernisation

wines.description: Fifth Growth Haut-Médoc revived since 2005 under the Merlaut-Villars family, located east of Saint-Julien. Cabernet-Merlot blends with a characteristic menthol freshness and improving quality from sustained investment.

wines.name: La Closerie de Camensac
wines.oak_regime: French oak barrels, lower proportion new wood than grand vin

taste.summary: The second wine of Château Camensac, prioritising freshness and supple tannins from younger vine selections. Light and immediately appealing with characteristic Haut-Médoc menthol freshness.

pairings.why: The wine's freshness and soft fruit complement simple grilled poultry with herbs.
The wine's lighter tannins and fresh acidity suit pork's delicate sweetness.

pairings.dish: Cheese board with mild soft cheeses
pairings.why: Gentle tannins and fruit forwardness pair with mild cheeses without overpowering.

history.summary: La Closerie de Camensac was developed as the second wine of Château Camensac from the time of the Merlaut-Villars family acquisition in 2005. It sources from younger vines and lots selected for freshness and supple texture rather than the structural ambition of the grand vin.

milestones.event: La Closerie de Camensac introduced under Merlaut-Villars ownership

wines.description: Second wine of Château de Camensac, introduced in 2005. Sourced from younger vines, emphasising fresh fruit and supple tannins for early drinkability. Good value Haut-Médoc.

wines.name: Chateau Canon
wines.classification: Saint-Emilion Premier Grand Cru Classe B (2022 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 18 months average in 50-60% new French oak barrels; malolactic fermentation primarily in stainless steel

taste.summary: Premier Grand Cru Classe B from the Saint-Emilion limestone plateau. Owned by the Wertheimer family (Chanel), Canon produces Merlot-dominant blends of great classical finesse and ageing capacity, with cedar, mint and graphite precision.

pairings.dish: Roasted leg of lamb with rosemary
pairings.why: Cedar and mint in the wine echo the herb notes; firm tannin structure integrates with lamb fat.

pairings.dish: Beef fillet with morel mushroom sauce
pairings.why: Earthy morel mushroom amplifies the wine's tobacco and graphite register; delicate beef fillet allows the wine's finesse to shine.

pairings.dish: Roasted pigeon with beet and blackberry
pairings.why: Game bird richness and fruit elements mirror the wine's red fruit and mineral structure.
Crystalline salt and nutty notes in aged Gruyere echo Canon's graphite and cedar minerality.

history.summary: Chateau Canon was established in the mid-18th century and has been one of Saint-Emilion's most consistent Premier Cru estates. Owned by the Wertheimer family (owners of Chanel) since 1996, the estate underwent extensive renovations. Under director Nicolas Audebert the estate has produced a series of brilliant vintages, most notably the 2015 and 2018-2022 runs. The classification as Premier Grand Cru Classe B was upheld in both the 2012 and 2022 revisions.

milestones.event: Estate established, originally called Saint-Martin de Mazerat
Wertheimer family (Chanel) acquires the estate
Awarded 100 points, hailed as legendary vintage
Premier Grand Cru Classe B status confirmed in reclassification

wines.description: Grand vin from Chateau Canon, Saint-Emilion Premier Grand Cru Classe B. Owned by the Wertheimer family (Chanel), producing Merlot-dominant blends of classical finesse and structure from the limestone plateau with cedar, mint and graphite precision.

wines.name: Croix Canon
wines.oak_regime: Aged in French oak barrels; proportion of new oak lower than grand vin

taste.finish: medium, fresh and lively with mint and cherry
taste.summary: Second wine of Chateau Canon (formerly Clos Canon, rebranded Croix Canon from the 2012 vintage). Elegant, medium-bodied with mint, cherry and floral character; structured enough for 10+ years but designed for earlier pleasure.

pairings.why: Mint and cherry register complements herb-roasted poultry without overpowering; elegant weight is a perfect match.
Gentle tannin and fresh acidity complement mild veal; floral notes echo herb accompaniments.
Lighter-bodied wine pairs with the earthy lentils and the rich-but-delicate salmon.

history.summary: The second wine of Chateau Canon was first produced in 2000 under the name Clos Canon, drawing on recently acquired parcels. Following the 2011 acquisition of Chateau Matras, the label was renamed Croix Canon from the 2012 vintage, in honor of the stone cross marking the entrance to the Mazerat chapel. The wine consistently receives strong critical recognition in its own right.

milestones.event: First vintage of Clos Canon, second wine of Chateau Canon
Acquisition of Chateau Matras adds parcels to second wine production
Renamed Croix Canon to honor the Mazerat chapel stone cross

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Canon, known as Clos Canon until the 2012 rebranding. Medium-bodied Merlot-Cabernet Franc blend with mint, cherry and floral elegance; accessible and structured for 10 or more years of drinking.

wines.name: Château Chasse-Spleen
wines.oak_regime: 40% new French oak barrels, 12-15 months

taste.summary: The benchmark Cru Bourgeois Exceptionnel of Moulis: Cabernet-dominant with dark fruit, pencil lead and licorice, offering genuine structure and ageing potential well beyond its humble classification.

pairings.dish: Roasted leg of lamb with garlic
pairings.why: The wine's structure and cassis fruit are a natural match for roasted lamb's mineral sweetness.
Classic Bordeaux pairing: the wine's dark fruit and medium grip suit bone-marrow-enriched Bordelaise.
Berry acidity in the sauce echoes the wine's cassis fruit while gamey venison suits Moulis structure.
Nutty, earthy cheese texture integrates the wine's tannins and amplifies its dark fruit.

history.summary: The Chasse-Spleen estate in Moulis has long been considered one of Bordeaux's most reliable over-performers. The name, meaning 'banish melancholy,' is attributed to a visit by Lord Byron. Under Céline Villars-Foubet and her family, the estate was elevated to Cru Bourgeois Exceptionnel status.

milestones.event: Modern estate era established in Moulis-en-Médoc
Second wines L'Oratoire and L'Ermitage de Chasse-Spleen introduced
Classified as Cru Bourgeois Exceptionnel in the revised classification

wines.description: The leading Cru Bourgeois Exceptionnel of Moulis-en-Médoc, celebrated for delivering first-rate structure and ageing potential far above its classification level. Named for the Byronic ideal of banishing melancholy.

wines.name: L'Héritage de Chasse-Spleen
wines.oak_regime: French oak, lighter extraction than grand vin

taste.summary: The Haut-Médoc AOC second-level wine of Chasse-Spleen, made from satellite vineyard parcels. Easy-drinking, herb-inflected red fruit for everyday occasions.

pairings.why: The wine's fresh red fruit and easy tannins pair naturally with a hearty meat ragu.
Soft tannins and fresh acidity are a relaxed match for grilled sausages.
Light structure and fruit forwardness complement a range of cured meats without effort.

history.summary: L'Héritage de Chasse-Spleen is produced from the estate's Haut-Médoc parcels, outside the Moulis appellation boundary. Introduced alongside L'Oratoire in 1982, it provides an even more accessible entry point to the Chasse-Spleen style.

milestones.event: L'Héritage de Chasse-Spleen introduced from Haut-Médoc satellite parcels

wines.description: Haut-Médoc AOC wine from Château Chasse-Spleen's satellite parcels, introduced in 1982. The most accessible and everyday of the Chasse-Spleen range.

wines.name: L'Oratoire de Chasse-Spleen
wines.oak_regime: French oak, lower proportion new wood than grand vin

taste.summary: The approachable second wine of Chasse-Spleen from the Moulis AOC: Merlot-driven, with enticing red fruit and soft tannins, ready to enjoy without extended cellaring.

pairings.why: The wine's Merlot-driven softness and red fruit complement the richness of duck confit.
Soft tannins and fresh fruit are a relaxed companion for slow-cooked or grilled pork ribs.
Gentle tannins and easy fruit forwardness pair without tension with milder cheeses.

history.summary: L'Oratoire de Chasse-Spleen was introduced in 1982 alongside L'Ermitage as one of the estate's second wine options, produced from the Moulis appellation parcels. Its higher Merlot proportion compared to the grand vin provides earlier drinkability.

milestones.event: L'Oratoire de Chasse-Spleen introduced as a second wine from Moulis parcels

wines.description: Second wine of Château Chasse-Spleen from Moulis-en-Médoc parcels. Merlot-dominant for early drinkability, with red fruit character and soft tannins. Good value Moulis.

wines.name: Chateau Cheval Blanc
wines.classification: Saint-Emilion Grand Cru (withdrew from Premier Grand Cru Classe A from 2022 vintage)
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 100% new French oak barrels

taste.summary: Cabernet Franc-dominant Saint-Emilion of breathtaking silkiness: violets and graphite on the nose, an opulent yet precise palate of black cherry and tobacco, seamless tannin structure and a finish of uncommon length.

pairings.dish: Roasted Pauillac lamb with herb crust
pairings.why: The wine's Cabernet Franc lift and graphite minerality cut through lanolin richness; herb notes mirror each other.
Earthy truffle amplifies the cedar-tobacco register; duck fat softens the wine's firm tannins.
Crystal salt and nuttiness in aged cheese mirror the wine's mocha and graphite finish.
Classic regional pairing: reduced red-wine sauce echoes the wine's own structure and dark-fruit core.

history.summary: Chateau Cheval Blanc has occupied its unique gravel-and-clay terroir on the western edge of Saint-Emilion since the mid-19th century, established after parcels were split from the Figeac estate. Renowned for the legendary 1947 vintage, it became one of the two original Premier Grand Cru Classe A estates in the 1955 Saint-Emilion classification. Purchased by Albert Frere and Baron Bernard Arnault in 1998, the estate underwent architectural renovation in 2011. In 2021 it announced withdrawal from the classification system from the 2022 vintage onward.

milestones.event: Estate formally established after separation from Chateau Figeac
The 1947 vintage became one of the most celebrated Bordeaux wines ever produced
Classified as Premier Grand Cru Classe A in the inaugural Saint-Emilion classification
Sold to Albert Frere and Bernard Arnault (LVMH)
Announced withdrawal from Saint-Emilion classification from 2022 vintage

wines.description: Cabernet Franc-dominant Saint-Emilion grand vin from Chateau Cheval Blanc, one of Bordeaux's most revered estates. The 1947 is considered a benchmark of the century; recent vintages achieve breathtaking silkiness and mineral precision.

wines.name: Le Petit Cheval
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 50% new French oak barrels

taste.summary: The second wine of Cheval Blanc: same terroir and typicity as the grand vin but in a more accessible, forward style. Charming black-fruit and cedar profile, silky tannins, and an earlier drinking window.

pairings.why: Medium-weight cherry fruit complements mild poultry without overwhelming it.
Earthy mushroom and soft veal echo the wine's cedar and herb register.
Rich duck fat integrates beautifully with the wine's soft tannin and plum fruit.

history.summary: Le Petit Cheval was created in 1988 as the second wine of Chateau Cheval Blanc, drawing on the same gravel-and-clay terroir as the grand vin. Typically sourced from younger vines and lighter-extraction parcels, it offers the estate's signature Cabernet Franc elegance at a more accessible entry point. Not produced in selected vintages where all production merges into the grand vin.

milestones.event: First vintage of Le Petit Cheval produced
No second wine produced; full crop elevated to grand vin

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Cheval Blanc, first produced in 1988. Draws on the same limestone and gravel terroir as the grand vin in a more supple, accessible style with Cabernet Franc finesse.

wines.name: Le Petit Cheval Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Barrel-fermented in 400-litre French oak; no malolactic fermentation

taste.summary: Barrel-fermented Sauvignon Blanc and Semillon blend from Cheval Blanc's estate, classified as Bordeaux Blanc since Saint-Emilion AOC prohibits white varieties. Elegant citrus and flint profile with saline length.

pairings.why: High acidity and mineral salinity are a textbook pairing with briny Atlantic oysters.
Citrus-led fruit echoes shellfish sweetness; acidity cuts through butter.
The wine's bright acidity contrasts the tangy cheese; herbal notes bridge the green salad.

history.summary: Le Petit Cheval Blanc was conceived as an experimental project from 2014, with the first commercial release being the 2015 vintage. White grape varieties are not permitted in the Saint-Emilion AOC, so the wine is classified as AOC Bordeaux Blanc. Initially 100% Sauvignon Blanc, the estate added Semillon from the 2018 vintage to introduce textural complexity.

milestones.event: First commercial vintage released as AOC Bordeaux Blanc
Semillon added to the blend for textural depth

wines.description: Barrel-fermented Sauvignon Blanc-Semillon blend from Chateau Cheval Blanc, first released in 2015 as AOC Bordeaux Blanc. Elegant, mineral-driven white with citrus precision and flint-salinity finish.

wines.name: Chateau Clinet
wines.oak_regime: 18-22 months in 60-80% new French oak barrels

taste.summary: Merlot-dominant Pomerol with an unusually significant proportion of Cabernet Sauvignon giving structured backbone. Rich and concentrated with cassis, graphite, and leather; requires a minimum of 8-12 years in good vintages.

pairings.dish: Entrecote with bone marrow and shallot sauce
pairings.why: Firm tannins cut through marrow richness; cassis and graphite bring classical Bordeaux affinity with aged beef.

pairings.dish: Wild boar or venison with berry sauce
pairings.why: The wine's dark fruit and leather notes find their mirror in gamey meats; the high acidity lifts the sauce.

pairings.dish: Roasted lamb shoulder with flageolet beans
pairings.why: Classic Right Bank pairing: Merlot fruit wraps around slow-roasted lamb while Cabernet Sauvignon brings a structural grip.
Firm tannic backbone is balanced by the salt and fat of aged hard cheese.

history.summary: Old maps record Clinet vines as early as 1785. The estate gained international recognition with the 1998 vintage, which received 100 points from Wine Advocate under the stewardship of the Laborde family. Ronan Laborde acquired the estate in 2004 and has since significantly raised quality. Production stands at approximately 3,200 cases per year from 9 hectares.

milestones.event: Estate formally established under the Clinet name
Wine Advocate awards 100 points, launching Clinet into the top tier of Pomerol
Ronan Laborde acquires the estate and becomes owner-winemaker

wines.description: Pomerol estate of 9 hectares with old-vine Merlot and a significant Cabernet Sauvignon component. 1998 vintage scored 100 from Wine Advocate; Ronan Laborde owner-winemaker since 2004.

wines.name: Fleur de Clinet
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 30% new, 70% second-fill French oak barrels

taste.summary: The accessible second wine of Clinet from estate vines, made to reveal its qualities early. Smooth-textured, fruit-driven Pomerol Merlot with approachable tannins and genuine ageing potential without requiring long cellaring.

pairings.why: Soft Merlot fruit and medium body complement mild poultry richness without overwhelming it.

pairings.dish: Boeuf aux carottes (braised beef and carrots)
pairings.why: The wine's smooth tannins and plum fruit work with slow-cooked beef and root vegetables.
Fresh acidity cuts through cured pork fat; plummy Merlot bridges rillettes and pate.

history.summary: Fleur de Clinet debuted in 1997 as the second wine of Chateau Clinet. Originally produced from declassified Clinet fruit, it is now sourced entirely from estate vines, reflecting Ronan Laborde's commitment to quality at every tier. It receives the same farming and vinification attention as the grand vin but in lighter-touch oak.

milestones.event: First vintage of Fleur de Clinet produced
Ronan Laborde refocuses production to estate vines only

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Clinet, first produced 1997, now from estate vines only. Approachable Merlot-dominant Pomerol aged in predominantly used oak; ready to drink young but with genuine ageing potential.

wines.name: Cos d'Estournel Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Barrel-fermented in combination of new and one-year-old French oak

taste.summary: One of the most prestigious white Bordeaux wines from Saint-Estephe, produced from a 10-hectare dedicated white wine parcel. Fresh, vibrant, and barrel-fermented; the Sauvignon Blanc drive and Semillon texture achieve remarkable complexity.

pairings.why: The wine's citrus acidity and Semillon richness cut through bisque richness while complementing delicate crustacean sweetness.
White fish with delicate flesh is lifted by Sauvignon Blanc's grass-and-citrus aromatics; oak adds weight without overpowering.
Creamy Norman cheese calls for the wine's citrus lift; oak structure bridges the richness.

history.summary: Cos d'Estournel Blanc was launched with the 2005 vintage to produce a premium white wine from a dedicated 10-hectare parcel, becoming one of the most sought-after white Bordeaux outside the Graves and Sauternes appellations. The combination of organic viticulture and barrel fermentation quickly established it as a reference point for white Medoc wines.

milestones.event: First vintage of Cos d'Estournel Blanc; white wine from dedicated 10-hectare parcel

wines.description: White wine of Cos d'Estournel since 2005, from 10 dedicated hectares. Sauvignon Blanc-dominant, barrel-fermented, one of the most prestigious white Bordeaux outside the classic appellations.

wines.name: Chateau Cos d'Estournel
wines.classification: Saint-Estephe AOC, Deuxieme Cru Classe (1855 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 50-60% new French oak barriques

taste.finish: epic length with spice, incense, and mineral tension
taste.summary: The most distinctive of the Saint-Estephe grands crus classés, with an unmistakable oriental spice and incense aromatic signature absent in Pauillac wines. Merlot-rich for Saint-Estephe, delivering seductive suppleness alongside the appellation's characteristic mineral-tannic power.

pairings.dish: Braised beef cheek with star anise and cinnamon
pairings.why: The wine's incense and spice aromatics mirror the Oriental spices in the braise; long-cooked collagen richness matches the wine's body.

pairings.dish: Roast leg of lamb with preserved lemon
pairings.why: Preserved lemon's acidity mirrors Cos d'Estournel's cassis and camphor; lamb fat is cut by the wine's firm tannin.

pairings.dish: Pigeon breast with foie gras and truffle
pairings.why: Luxurious combination that matches Cos d'Estournel's concentration and aromatic complexity in its greatest vintages.
Wild game accentuates the wine's dark fruit and incense register; elderberry echoes the floral notes on the nose.

history.summary: Founded by Louis-Gaspard d'Estournel in 1810, the estate is named for the hill (Cos or Caux in Gascon) on which it sits. D'Estournel built the famous pagoda-adorned chai inspired by his travels to India and China, where he had exported wine. The estate passed through various owners before Michel Reybier acquired it in 2000. Fully organic since 2024.

milestones.event: Founded by Louis-Gaspard d'Estournel
Classified as Deuxieme Cru Classe in the Medoc classification
Acquired by Michel Reybier; major investment and quality elevation
Wine Advocate 100 points; James Suckling 100 points
Full Ecocert organic certification achieved across all 91 hectares

wines.description: Saint-Estephe second growth with a unique incense and oriental spice aromatic signature. Merlot-rich for the appellation; Ecocert certified since 2024. The 2016 vintage earned perfect scores from Wine Advocate and James Suckling.

wines.name: Les Pagodes de Cos
wines.oak_regime: 40% new French oak barriques

taste.finish: medium-long with dark fruit and minerality
taste.summary: Second wine of Cos d'Estournel since 1994; previously sold as Marbuzet. Shows a more Merlot-forward profile than the grand vin, with the estate's distinctive spice character in a more accessible format.

pairings.dish: Lamb tagine with apricots and preserved lemon
pairings.why: The wine's soft spice tones and dark fruit echo North African aromatics; lamb is the natural partner.
Plum notes in the wine mirror the sauce; duck fat is balanced by firm structure.
Earthy mushroom and herbs connect with the wine's mineral and cedar register.

history.summary: The second wine of Cos d'Estournel was originally sold as Marbuzet. With the 1994 vintage it was rebranded as Les Pagodes de Cos, named after the estate's iconic pagoda-adorned chai. The wine is made from 40-year-old vines not selected for the grand vin.

milestones.event: Rebranded from Marbuzet to Les Pagodes de Cos with this vintage

wines.description: Second wine of Cos d'Estournel since 1994 (formerly Marbuzet). Merlot-forward expression of the estate's spice and mineral character at an accessible price.

wines.name: Aria du Chateau de la Riviere
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 100% new French oak barrels

taste.summary: The prestige selection of Chateau de la Riviere, sourced exclusively from the estate's oldest Merlot vines in a single 1.5-hectare plot and aged entirely in new French oak. Produced only in the best vintages; a luxury expression of Fronsac terroir.

pairings.why: The wine's concentrated dark fruit and vanillin oak are a natural partner to duxelles-wrapped fillet; the richness of pastry is matched by Aria's full body.

pairings.dish: Slow-roasted short rib with truffle mash
pairings.why: Collagen richness and earthy truffle resonate with Aria's mocha and cedar register.
Full-body and plush fruit cushion the intense fat of foie gras; a rare Left-meets-Right pairing.

history.summary: The Aria cuvee was created by the estate in 2000 as a luxury selection from the oldest Merlot vines on the 1.5-hectare plateau plot. Produced only in superior vintages with 15,000 bottles maximum, it is the reference wine showing what Fronsac can achieve at maximum concentration. The 100% new oak regime distinguishes it from the estate's regular cuvee.

milestones.event: First vintage of Aria produced as prestige selection from oldest vines

wines.description: Prestige Fronsac selection from Chateau de la Riviere's oldest Merlot vines in a single 1.5-hectare plot. 100% new French oak; produced only in best vintages; first vintage 2000.

wines.name: Le Blanc de Chateau de la Riviere
wines.oak_regime: 10% in new oak barrels; 90% stainless steel on lees for 4 months

taste.summary: An unusual Bordeaux Blanc built on Sauvignon Gris rather than Semillon, giving tropical and citrus character with a rich, textured palate. Only 5,600 bottles produced annually; fermented mostly in stainless steel to preserve freshness.

pairings.dish: Grilled sea bass with lemon butter
pairings.why: Sauvignon Blanc acidity cuts through butter; citrus aromas amplify the lemon.
The wine's herbal freshness and acidity lift the creaminess of the cheese.
Crisp, citrus-forward white with natural acidity is a classic pairing with mussels in white wine.

history.summary: The white wine of Chateau de la Riviere was introduced as a modern expression of Bordeaux Blanc, unusually dominated by Sauvignon Gris alongside Sauvignon Blanc. Production is deliberately limited to 5,600 bottles to ensure quality from the single plot.

milestones.event: First vintage of Blanc de la Riviere produced using Sauvignon Gris-dominant blend

wines.description: Limited-production Bordeaux Blanc from Chateau de la Riviere; 66% Sauvignon Blanc, 34% Sauvignon Gris. Only 5,600 bottles; mostly stainless steel. Vivid, textured, and tropical.

wines.name: Chateau de la Riviere
wines.oak_regime: 15 months in French oak: 40% new, 60% once-used barrels

taste.summary: The flagship Fronsac estate red from 60 hectares on clay-limestone soils above the Dordogne. Merlot-led and supple with dark chocolate and ripe cherry; aged in spectacular 16th-century underground cellars at constant 13 degrees.

pairings.why: Duck breast richness is lifted by the wine's ripe cherry and dark fruit character; spice complements the sauce.

pairings.dish: Pork shoulder with prunes and Armagnac
pairings.why: Sweet-savory braised pork and dried fruit resonate with the wine's plum and chocolate register.

pairings.dish: Grilled entrecote with green peppercorn sauce
pairings.why: Velvety tannins cushion the steak; the wine's licorice note echoes the pepper.

pairings.dish: Mushroom and walnut salad with Roquefort
pairings.why: Earthy mushroom and pungent cheese find counterpoint in the wine's round, supple texture.

history.summary: Chateau de la Riviere is one of the oldest estates in Fronsac, with records dating to the 16th century. The property's spectacular cave network, carved into the limestone cliffs above the Dordogne, was used for ageing wine from the earliest vintages. Xavier Buffo acquired the estate in 2003 and engaged Michel Rolland as consulting winemaker, elevating quality to regional leadership.

milestones.event: Estate established; limestone cave network used for wine storage
Xavier Buffo acquires the estate; Michel Rolland engaged as consultant
Decanter awards 93 points, highest published score for Fronsac at time

wines.description: Fronsac flagship from 60 hectares of clay-limestone, Michel Rolland consulting. 84% Merlot aged in historic 16th-century underground cellars. Consistent overperformer at accessible Fronsac prices.

wines.name: Château Ducru-Beaucaillou
wines.oak_regime: 75-90% new French oak barrels, 18-20 months

taste.summary: Archetype of refined Saint-Julien: cassis and dark cherry on a bed of Gunzian gravel minerality, with perfectly integrated silky tannins and an exceptionally lengthy finish.

pairings.dish: Rack of Pauillac lamb with herb crust
pairings.why: The wine's firm tannin structure and cedar notes harmonise with the lanolin richness of local lamb.
Crystalline mountain-cheese salt sharpens the wine's cassis fruit without overwhelming it.

pairings.dish: Roasted wood pigeon with truffle jus
pairings.why: Gamey, earthy register of pigeon mirrors the wine's tobacco and graphite notes.
Classic Bordeaux pairing: the wine's dark fruit and firm grip cut the marbling of a rib-eye.

history.summary: The estate traces its modern identity to 1720 when it was established as a family property in the Médoc. The distinctive name references the Gunzian quartz-pebble soils ('beaux cailloux') along the Gironde. The Borie family acquired it in 1941 and elevated it to super-second status through rigorous selection.

milestones.event: Property established as a Médoc wine estate
Classified Deuxième Cru in the Bordeaux 1855 Classification
Borie family purchase and quality revival begins
Second wine La Croix Ducru-Beaucaillou introduced

wines.description: The quintessential Saint-Julien Deuxième Cru, grown on iron-rich Gunzian gravel soils beside the Gironde estuary. Borie family-owned since 1941, producing Cabernet-dominant blends of remarkable finesse and longevity.

wines.name: La Croix Ducru-Beaucaillou
wines.oak_regime: 60% new French oak barrels

taste.summary: Powerful and silky with aromatic richness; introduces the Borie house style at an accessible level with plush berry fruit and balanced oak spice.

pairings.why: The wine's Merlot-forward softness and dark fruit complement the mineral sweetness of milk-fed lamb.

pairings.dish: Duck confit with roasted root vegetables
pairings.why: Rich, gamey duck fat meets the wine's subtly spicy cassis and plush tannins in harmony.
Umami-rich mushrooms amplify the wine's earthy, tobacco-inflected backbone.

history.summary: Created in 1995 as the estate's second wine, La Croix Ducru-Beaucaillou sources from younger vines and parcels that do not meet the strict selection for the grand vin. It took its current name in 2019, replacing the previous 'La Croix de Beaucaillou' label.

milestones.event: Second wine launched under the name La Croix de Beaucaillou
Renamed to La Croix Ducru-Beaucaillou with updated label

wines.description: Second wine of Château Ducru-Beaucaillou introduced in 1995, offering the Borie house style of silky texture and aromatic complexity at a more accessible price point.

wines.name: Chateau d'Yquem
wines.oak_regime: 100% new French oak; 30 months aging

taste.summary: The world's most celebrated botrytised sweet wine; the sole Premier Cru Superieur in the 1855 Sauternes classification. Semillon-dominant with intense noble-rot character: beeswax, saffron, apricot, and honey balanced by electric acidity. Ages for decades and improves for 50 or more years in great vintages.

pairings.why: The great Sauternes-foie gras pairing: the wine's honeyed sweetness and high acidity cut through richness and amplify the luxurious texture.
Salty, pungent blue cheese is transformed by the wine's honey and apricot character; sweetness tempers sharpness.
Caramelised sugar in the brulee echoes the wine's caramel and beeswax notes; sweetness levels align.
High residual sugar and bright acidity balance the heat; honey and tropical fruit notes mirror coconut and lemongrass.
Stone fruit in the wine amplifies fresh peach; the pastry's butteriness aligns with beeswax notes.
Crystalline, nutty Comté amplifies the wine's toasted hazelnut and caramelised notes while salt cuts through sweetness.

history.summary: Chateau d'Yquem was the first wine from France to be designated a world first-growth in the 1855 classification, receiving the unique ranking of Premier Cru Superieur. The estate dates to the 12th century but noble-rot wine production was established in 1666. The Lur-Saluces family managed the estate for over 400 years until 1999, when LVMH acquired a majority stake. Lorenzo Pasquini became director-general in 2019, overseeing the conversion to 100% organic farming in 2022.

milestones.event: Noble rot botrytised wine production established at the estate
Classified Premier Cru Superieur -- the only estate to hold this unique rank above Premier Cru Classe
Y de Yquem dry white first produced
LVMH acquires majority control after decades of Lur-Saluces family management
First certified organic vintage

wines.description: The world's sole Premier Cru Superieur Sauternes. 100% new French oak, 30-month ageing on a Semillon-Sauvignon Blanc blend harvested berry by berry through multiple passes. Beeswax, saffron, apricot, and honey with extraordinary acidity; ages magnificently for 50-plus years.

wines.name: Y de Yquem
wines.oak_regime: 50% new French oak; 10 months

taste.summary: The singular dry white wine of Chateau d'Yquem, made only in years when the estate decides against a botrytised grand vin or in addition to it. Sauvignon Blanc-dominant, barrel-fermented in 50% new oak, offering honeyed citrus, white peach, and mineral depth unique to the Sauternes terroir.

pairings.why: Full body and honeyed richness complement the buttery sauce; high acidity keeps the match lively.
Sweet crustacean and herbed butter partner the wine's stone fruit and herbal character.
The wine's honeyed weight and mineral depth interact dramatically with truffle earthiness.
Saffron in the dish echoes the terroir character of the wine; sweet scallop complements citrus fruit.

history.summary: Y de Yquem (pronounced 'Ygrec', meaning the letter Y in French) was first produced in 1959 when harvest conditions in Sauternes did not favour botrytis. The estate decided to produce a dry white wine from the same Semillon and Sauvignon Blanc vines. It is made only in certain years and is considered one of the most unusual and sought-after dry whites in France, produced from the same celebrated terroir as the grand vin.

milestones.event: Y de Yquem first produced; dry white wine from the Sauternes estate
Regular annual production of Y de Yquem begins, though production varies by vintage

wines.description: The dry white alter ego of Chateau d'Yquem, first made in 1959. Sauvignon Blanc-dominant, barrel-fermented in 50% new oak from the same Sauternes terroir as the world's greatest botrytised wine. Produced in limited quantities only in certain vintages.

wines.name: Chateau Figeac
wines.classification: Saint-Emilion Premier Grand Cru Classe A (2022 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 14-18 months in 100% new French oak; malolactic fermentation in barrels since 2009

taste.summary: The most Cabernet-dominant Right Bank premier cru in Bordeaux: near-equal thirds of Cabernet Franc, Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot. Promoted to Premier Grand Cru Classe A in 2022. Combines peony and iris fragrance with electric energy, grainy Cabernet tannin and remarkable mineral length.

pairings.dish: Roasted Pauillac lamb with garlic and thyme
pairings.why: The wine's Cabernet-driven structure and high acidity cut through lamb fat; graphite and pencil-lead notes mirror classic herb seasoning.

pairings.dish: Magret de canard with black currant sauce
pairings.why: Cassis and raspberry fruit echo the sauce; the wine's electric acidity refreshes the rich duck breast.
Game intensity and rich pasta demand the wine's Cabernet backbone and dark-fruit density.

pairings.dish: Black truffle and brie en croute
pairings.why: Earthy truffle amplifies the wine's forest-floor and cigar-wrapper register; melted brie softens the firm tannins.

history.summary: Chateau Figeac was carved from larger Figeac estate lands in the 19th century and shares its gravel terroir with Cheval Blanc to the west. The Manoncourt family managed the estate from 1947-2010 under Thierry Manoncourt, who pioneered Right Bank quality standards including temperature-controlled fermentation (1971) and the first second wine on the Right Bank. The estate was promoted from Premier Grand Cru Classe B to Premier Grand Cru Classe A in the landmark 2022 reclassification. Current CEO is Frederic Faye.

milestones.event: Thierry Manoncourt takes charge; quality transformation of the modern era begins
First Right Bank estate to install temperature-controlled fermentation
Promoted to Premier Grand Cru Classe A in the Saint-Emilion reclassification

wines.description: Grand vin from Chateau Figeac, promoted to Premier Grand Cru Classe A in 2022. The most Cabernet-dominant Right Bank first-growth, with near-equal thirds of Cabernet Franc, Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot delivering electric freshness, peony fragrance and extraordinary mineral length.

wines.name: Petit-Figeac
wines.oak_regime: Aged in French oak barrels; same approach as grand vin but from younger vines

taste.summary: The second wine of Chateau Figeac, one of the oldest second wines on the Right Bank (first produced 1945). Shares the same Cabernet-influenced gravel terroir and blending trio as the grand vin but from younger vines; lighter, more refined and more accessible earlier.

pairings.why: Medium Cabernet structure complements poultry elegantly; earthy mushroom mirrors the wine's mineral notes.

pairings.dish: Grilled veal cutlet with sage butter
pairings.why: Elegant high acidity and red fruit work with mild veal; sage provides herbal bridge.

pairings.dish: Lamb shoulder with tomatoes and olives
pairings.why: Vibrant acidity integrates with acidic tomato; the wine's Cabernet firmness lifts the rich lamb.

history.summary: Petit-Figeac is one of Bordeaux's oldest second wines, created by Thierry Manoncourt in 1945 when he initiated strict quality-based selection across the Figeac estate. Originally sold under various names (La Grange Neuve de Figeac in some eras), it was formally renamed Petit-Figeac from the 2013 vintage. The wine is made exclusively from Figeac estate grapes, following the same Cabernet-heavy philosophy as the grand vin.

milestones.event: Thierry Manoncourt creates the first Right Bank second wine at Figeac
Formally renamed Petit-Figeac

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Figeac, one of the oldest second wines on the Right Bank, first produced in 1945. Cabernet-influenced Merlot blend from younger estate vines; lighter and more accessible than the grand vin while retaining Figeac's distinctive high-acidity mineral precision.

wines.name: Chateau Haut-Brion
wines.classification: Premier Cru Classe (1855 classification); Cru Classe de Graves
wines.oak_regime: Up to 100% new French oak, 22 to 24 months; approximately 70% of barrels made on-site at the estate cooperage

taste.summary: Merlot-led first-growth Pessac-Leognan red combining cassis and graphite with earthy tobacco and cedar. Velvety tannins and exceptional length set it apart from Medoc peers.

pairings.dish: Roasted Pauillac lamb with thyme jus
pairings.why: The earthy graphite and tobacco character of the wine echoes the herbal notes of thyme; firm tannins cut through the lamb fat.
Full body and cassis fruit stand up to the richness of marrow; scorched-earth notes bridge the char on the meat.
Crystalline salt and nuttiness in aged hard cheese sharpens the wine's red-fruit core.
Earthy truffle amplifies the wine's graphite and cedar register; the creamy sweetbreads soften the firm tannins.
Duck fat and citrus glaze find an anchor in the wine's dark-fruit core while firm tannins cut through richness.

history.summary: Chateau Haut-Brion is the only first growth outside the Medoc, classified in 1855 alongside the four Pauillac estates. The property has been documented under that name since 1521 and was the first Bordeaux wine to be exported to England in the 17th century. Acquired in 1935 by American diplomat Clarence Dillon, it has been managed by the Dillon family ever since, with Jean-Philippe Delmas directing viticulture and winemaking since 2003.

milestones.event: Estate first documented by the Pontac family
Samuel Pepys records tasting 'Ho Bryan' at the Royal Oak Tavern, London -- the first named Bordeaux wine tasted in England
Classified Premier Cru Classe in the Bordeaux Classification, the only non-Medoc estate to achieve first-growth rank
Acquired by American banker Clarence Dillon; estate subsequently modernised
Temperature-controlled double-skinned stainless steel vats introduced, replacing traditional oak fermenters

wines.description: The sole first-growth outside the Medoc, Haut-Brion has been produced since at least 1521. Merlot-dominant blends aged in up to 100% new French oak deliver cassis, graphite, and tobacco with extraordinary longevity.

wines.name: Chateau Haut-Brion Blanc
wines.oak_regime: 100% new French oak; barrel-fermented; 13 to 16 months on lees; no malolactic fermentation

taste.summary: One of the world's rarest and most sought-after dry whites, Haut-Brion Blanc blends Semillon richness with Sauvignon Blanc precision. White peach, orange blossom, and mineral salinity build to a honeyed, lingering finish.

pairings.why: The creamy butter sauce and sweet crustacean flesh find a counterpoint in the wine's racy acidity and citrus lift.
Delicate white fish and emulsified butter echo the wine's smooth texture and nectarine fruit without overwhelming it.
Herbal freshness and shellfish sweetness align with the wine's floral and mineral character.
Earthy truffle and sweet scallop bring out the saffron and stone notes in the wine's palate.

history.summary: White wine has been produced at Haut-Brion for centuries, but the modern bottling dates its line from the 1959 vintage. Production is tiny -- around 600 cases per year -- making it one of the rarest white Bordeaux in existence. Classified as a Cru Classe de Graves for whites since 1959, it consistently rivals the world's finest dry whites.

milestones.event: Modern era of Chateau Haut-Brion Blanc established; white Graves classification created
La Clarte de Haut-Brion introduced as the second white wine, replacing Les Plantiers du Haut-Brion

wines.description: Among the world's most scarce and coveted dry whites. Barrel-fermented Semillon-Sauvignon Blanc from Pessac-Leognan, produced in only around 600 cases per year with extraordinary mineral depth and ageing potential.

wines.name: Le Clarence de Haut-Brion
wines.oak_regime: Approximately 30% new French oak, 18 months

taste.summary: The second red of Haut-Brion, renamed from Bahans Haut-Brion in 2007. Merlot-dominated in most vintages with the same Pessac terroir as the grand vin, but more approachable in youth, showing red plum, blueberry, and mineral freshness.

pairings.why: The wine's plum-and-anise profile complements duck fat while Merlot softness makes it approachable alongside earthy lentils.

pairings.dish: Roast chicken with thyme and garlic
pairings.why: Fresh red fruit and moderate tannin are classic partners for roast poultry; the wine's weight matches the dish.
Forest floor and earthy notes in the wine echo the umami depth of mushrooms.
Earthy rind and semi-firm paste interact gently with the wine's soft tannins and berry fruit.

history.summary: Originally called Bahans Haut-Brion when introduced in 1976, the second red wine of Chateau Haut-Brion was renamed Le Clarence de Haut-Brion in honour of Clarence Dillon, who purchased the estate in 1935. From the 2007 vintage onward it has carried this name and is produced from younger vines and declassified lots of the grand vin.

milestones.event: Second wine introduced under the name Bahans Haut-Brion
Renamed Le Clarence de Haut-Brion in honour of founder Clarence Dillon

wines.description: The second red wine of Haut-Brion, called Bahans until 2006 and renamed Le Clarence in 2007. Merlot-forward with the same Pessac-Leognan gravel terroir as the grand vin, offering immediate dark-fruit pleasure with a structured finish.

wines.name: Chateau Lafaurie-Peyraguey
wines.oak_regime: Average 60% new French oak barrels; 18 to 20 months

taste.summary: Among the finest Premier Cru Classe Sauternes, with an exceptionally high Semillon content (up to 100% in some vintages) that delivers an unusually pure, concentrated expression of noble rot. Pineapple, candied citrus, honeycomb, and powdered ginger with vivid acidity; the 2013 renaissance under Silvio Denz transformed quality.

pairings.dish: Foie gras au torchon with brioche
pairings.why: The Sauternes-foie gras pairing at its most opulent; honeycomb and candied citrus cut through the richness.
Pungent blue cheese and sweet fig resonate with the wine's candied-citrus and honeycomb character; acidity balances salt.
Orange peel and caramelised sugar in the dessert mirror the wine's tangerine preserves and creme brulee palate note.
Tropical pineapple in the wine amplifies the tart; ginger spice echoes the powdered-ginger aroma note.
High residual sugar and bright acidity tame chilli heat; tropical fruit and ginger notes mirror key curry aromatics.

history.summary: Chateau Lafaurie-Peyraguey sits on a hilltop in Bommes facing Chateau d'Yquem across the Sauternes valley, classified Premier Cru in 1855. Owned by Cordier from 1913 to 2013, the estate languished in mediocrity before being acquired by Silvio Denz in 2013. Denz invested substantially in a new winery (opened 2018) and built the Lalique hotel and restaurant on the estate, dramatically raising quality. Lalique Group acquired a 75% majority stake in 2023.

milestones.event: Classified Premier Cru Classe in the Sauternes-Barsac classification
Cordier family acquires the estate
Silvio Denz purchases the estate; quality transformation begins
New winery and Lalique hotel and restaurant open on the estate
Lalique Group acquires 75% majority stake

wines.description: Premier Cru Classe Sauternes from Bommes, revitalised after Silvio Denz's 2013 acquisition. Up to 100% Semillon in some vintages; extraordinary concentration of candied citrus, honeycomb, and powdered ginger with electric acidity.

wines.name: La Chapelle de Lafaurie-Peyraguey
wines.oak_regime: French oak barrels; proportion of new oak lower than grand vin; 18 months

taste.summary: The second sweet wine of Lafaurie-Peyraguey, sharing the estate's Bommes terroir and high Semillon content. White peach, apricot, and lemon cream notes lead a direct, energetic palate with mellow sweetness balanced by lively acidity. More accessible in youth than the grand vin.

pairings.why: The classic pairing scaled to an approachable price point; the wine's sweetness and acidity manage the richness elegantly.
Stone fruit in wine and tart resonate; almond cream mirrors the wine's subtle nuttiness.
Blue cheese and walnut sweetness find a counterpoint in the wine's apricot acidity and honey.

history.summary: La Chapelle de Lafaurie-Peyraguey is the second Sauternes wine of the estate. It draws from younger vines and lots not selected for the grand vin and is aged in a lower proportion of new oak. The 2013 vintage earned a Platinum award at the Decanter World Wine Awards, reflecting the estate's revitalisation under Silvio Denz.

milestones.event: La Chapelle de Lafaurie-Peyraguey introduced as the estate's second Sauternes wine
La Chapelle wins Platinum at Decanter World Wine Awards under new Denz ownership

wines.description: Second Sauternes wine of Chateau Lafaurie-Peyraguey. High Semillon content from Bommes; fresh white peach, apricot, and lemon cream notes with lively acidity. More approachable in youth than the grand vin.

wines.name: Le Lys de Lafaurie-Peyraguey
wines.oak_regime: Partial barrel fermentation; stainless steel component; 10 months

taste.summary: A dry white Bordeaux introduced in 2014 from the Lafaurie-Peyraguey estate in Bommes. Sauvignon Blanc-dominant with Semillon adding body; white flowers, lemon blossom, and fresh citrus lead a mineral, herb-tinged palate. An accessible entry into the Sauternes estate's terroir as a dry expression.

pairings.why: Crisp acidity and herbal notes complement delicate fish; the mineral drive cleanses each bite.
Classic Sauvignon Blanc and goat cheese pairing; the wine's citrus acidity amplifies the fresh cheese flavour.
High acidity and herbal freshness align naturally with asparagus; vinaigrette's tartness is echoed in the wine.

history.summary: Le Lys de Lafaurie-Peyraguey was introduced in 2014, the year after Silvio Denz acquired the estate, as a dry white wine that offers an accessible entry to the Bommes terroir. It demonstrates the Sauternes estate's flexibility in producing both sweet and dry wines from its exceptional Semillon and Sauvignon Blanc plantings.

milestones.event: Le Lys de Lafaurie-Peyraguey introduced as the estate's first dry white wine

wines.description: Dry white wine from Chateau Lafaurie-Peyraguey, introduced in 2014. Sauvignon Blanc-dominant from Bommes terroir; white flowers, lemon blossom, and mineral freshness. The estate's accessible dry white expression.

wines.name: Carruades de Lafite
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 50-55% new French oak barriques

taste.summary: The second wine of Lafite shares the elegance and lead-pencil signature of the grand vin but with a higher Merlot component lending earlier approachability. Floral aromatic lift distinguishes it from heavier second wines.

pairings.why: The wine's red fruit and tobacco notes mirror duck's savouriness; lentils provide earthiness that anchors the cassis core.
Richness of long-braised beef is cut by Carruades' still-firm tannin structure.

pairings.dish: Lamb shoulder with garlic and rosemary
pairings.why: Herbal notes in the wine's bouquet align with Mediterranean aromatics; moderate tannins match the texture of slow-cooked lamb.

history.summary: The estate produced a second wine under the name Moulin des Carruades in the mid-19th century but discontinued the practice. The modern second wine was revived with the 1966 vintage as Moulin de Carruades. The name was shortened to Carruades de Lafite in 1979. The Carruades plateau is one of the highest-elevation gravel terroirs on the Left Bank.

milestones.event: Modern second wine revived as Moulin de Carruades
Renamed Carruades de Lafite
Sourced entirely from certified organic vineyards following estate-wide Ecocert transition

wines.description: Second wine of Lafite Rothschild, first revived in 1966. Higher Merlot proportion than the grand vin gives earlier approachability while retaining the estate's signature cedar and graphite character.

wines.name: Chateau Lafite Rothschild
wines.classification: Pauillac AOC, Premier Cru Classe (1855 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 18 to 20 months in 100% new French oak barriques

taste.finish: very long with graphite and cedar
taste.summary: The archetype of Pauillac first-growth: Cabernet-dominant, with penetrating lead pencil and cassis aromatics, precise fine-grained tannins, and a finish that evolves over decades.

pairings.dish: Roast rack of lamb with herbs de Provence
pairings.why: The wine's grippy tannins and cassis core cut the rich fat of lamb while the herbal notes find an echo.
The crystalline salt of aged hard cheese amplifies Lafite's cedar and pencil shaving register.
The local AOC lamb is classically paired with first-growth Pauillac; the wine's structure mirrors the lamb's sweet, fine-grained flesh.
Black truffle's earthy depth resonates with Lafite's mushroom and graphite tones in mature vintages.

history.summary: Records of Lafite Rothschild winemaking stretch to the 17th century; the estate was already renowned at Versailles in the 18th century. The Rothschild family acquired it in 1868. The oldest surviving confirmed bottle dates to 1797. The grand vin is one of five original Premier Cru Classe wines in the 1855 Medoc classification.

milestones.event: Classified as one of four original Premier Cru Classe in the Medoc classification (alongside Latour, Margaux, and Haut-Brion)
Purchased by Baron James Mayer de Rothschild at auction
Estate achieves full organic certification (Ecocert) across all 112 hectares under Saskia de Rothschild

wines.description: Archetypal Pauillac first-growth from 112 hectares on gravelly soil. Cabernet-dominant blend with 18 to 20 months in 100% new oak; one of the longest-lived wines in Bordeaux.

wines.name: Chateau Lafleur
wines.oak_regime: 15 months in 25% new French oak; 75% previously used barrels from Grand Village

taste.summary: Unique Pomerol with an equal Cabernet Franc-Merlot split giving a perfumed floral lift and structural precision rarely seen in the appellation. Biodynamic farming since 2014; produced from just 4.5 hectares on the Pomerol plateau.

pairings.dish: Roasted rack of venison with wild berry reduction
pairings.why: The wine's high Cabernet Franc percentage gives a tension and berry brightness that lifts the gamey meat.
Black truffle aromas and the wine's deep concentration find a natural pairing; tannin structure handles the fat.
Lanolin richness of aged sheep's milk harmonises with the wine's floral aromatics.

pairings.dish: Slow-braised short rib with root vegetables
pairings.why: Collagen-rich beef gains complexity from the iron-mineral core; fruit depth matches the long braise.

history.summary: Lafleur dates to 1872 and passed through several owners before the Guinaudeau family took charge. Baptiste and Julie Guinaudeau manage the tiny 4.5-hectare estate with a precision that has earned it comparisons to Petrus. Biodynamic farming was introduced in 2014, aligning with the family's philosophy of minimal intervention. The 25% new oak regime is deliberately conservative for a wine of this quality level.

milestones.event: Estate established on the Pomerol plateau
Guinaudeau family first acquires an interest in the property
First vintage of Pensees de Lafleur sister wine produced
Biodynamic farming adopted across the estate

wines.description: Tiny 4.5-hectare Pomerol with a unique 50% Cabernet Franc, 50% Merlot blend. Guinaudeau family; biodynamic since 2014; considered peer to Petrus. Conservative oak regime: 25% new wood.

wines.name: Les Pensees de Lafleur
wines.oak_regime: 15 months in French oak, lower proportion new wood than the grand vin

taste.summary: Produced from a separate 0.8-hectare crescent parcel with deeper soils, Les Pensees is not a second wine but a distinct expression of the Lafleur terroir. More Merlot-forward than the grand vin, with the same biodynamic farming and iron-mineral character.

pairings.why: Medium body and red cherry fruit complement rich confit duck; earthy lentils echo the iron-mineral notes.
Cabernet Franc floral notes match the herbal brightness of spring vegetables; Merlot fruit wraps the lamb.
Earthy mushroom and fresh goats cheese match the Pomerol mineral edge and soft fruit.

history.summary: Created in 1984 by the Guinaudeau family as an independent expression from a separate parcel rather than a declassified second wine. Originally called Pensees de Lafleur; renamed Les Pensees from the 2018 vintage to better reflect its distinct terroir identity. Produced in small volumes of approximately 250 cases per year.

milestones.event: First vintage of Pensees de Lafleur produced as a separate wine
Renamed to Les Pensees to signal distinct terroir identity

wines.description: Sister wine to Chateau Lafleur from a distinct 0.8-hectare parcel; produced since 1984 and renamed Les Pensees from 2018. Not a second wine; reflects its own deeper-soil terroir with more Merlot and the same biodynamic farming.

wines.name: Château Larose-Trintaudon
wines.oak_regime: 20% new French oak barrels, 12 months

taste.summary: The largest single vineyard in the Médoc produces an consistently approachable, fruit-forward Haut-Médoc with a well-balanced structure and excellent everyday drinkability at an accessible price.

pairings.why: The wine's cassis fruit and medium grip are a classic Bordelais match for a simple grilled steak.
Approachable tannins and ripe berry fruit complement the mild richness of roasted poultry.
Herbed lamb patties are elevated by the wine's cassis and subtle cedar.

history.summary: Vines were first planted on the Trintaudon parcel in 1838. The estate grew to become one of the Médoc's largest single vineyards at over 210 hectares, famous for its ship-like silhouette visible above the vines. Classified Cru Bourgeois Supérieur in 2020.

milestones.event: First vines planted on the Trintaudon parcel
ISO 14001 environmental certification achieved
Classified as Cru Bourgeois Supérieur

wines.description: The largest single estate vineyard in the Médoc at over 210 hectares. Cru Bourgeois Supérieur offering consistently approachable, fruit-driven Haut-Médoc at one of Bordeaux's best price-to-quality ratios.

wines.name: Les Hauts de Trintaudon
wines.oak_regime: Short oak contact, lower proportion new wood than grand vin

taste.summary: The accessible second wine of Larose-Trintaudon, sourced from the same vines and terroir as the grand vin but crafted for early approachability and everyday enjoyment.

pairings.why: The wine's soft tannins and juicy berry fruit are a relaxed partner for simple grilled sausages.
Bright acidity and light fruit complement tomato-based pizzas without competing.
Soft tannins and fresh structure make this an easy-drinking companion to cured meats.

history.summary: The second wine of Château Larose-Trintaudon, first produced around 1990, sourced from the same exceptional gravel soils of the Saint-Laurent-Médoc but crafted for earlier accessibility. The name 'Les Hauts de Trintaudon' refers to the higher elevations of the estate.

milestones.event: Les Hauts de Trintaudon introduced as the estate's second wine

wines.description: Second wine of Château Larose-Trintaudon, made from the same vines and terroir as the grand vin but crafted for immediate approachability. Excellent-value everyday Haut-Médoc.

wines.name: Chateau Latour
wines.classification: Pauillac AOC, Premier Cru Classe (1855 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 100% new French oak barriques

taste.finish: extraordinary length with pencil and stone
taste.summary: The most structured and austere of the Pauillac first-growths. The L'Enclos parcel of old-vine Cabernet Sauvignon delivers extraordinary concentration and mineral tension; legendary longevity requires patience of decades.

pairings.dish: Entrecote bordelaise with bone marrow and shallot sauce
pairings.why: The wine's iron-firm tannins cut the fat; bone marrow richness mirrors the wine's weight.
Regional pairing: the local milk-fed lamb's delicacy matches Latour's precise tannin structure better than heavier cuts.
In mature vintages, Latour develops truffle-and-earth tones that resonate with white truffle oil and funky aged cheese.
Forest-floor aromatics in old-vintage Latour are echoed by earthy mushrooms; rich pastry provides structure.

history.summary: The fortress tower at the tip of Pauillac appears in records from the 14th century. Modern estate history begins in the 17th century with the Segur family. Sold to a British-French consortium in 1963 and then to Francois Pinault's Groupe Artemis in 1993. Latour controversially withdrew from the en primeur system in 2012, releasing wines only when deemed ready.

milestones.event: Classified Premier Cru Classe in the Medoc classification
Sold to a consortium of British and French investors; Harveys of Bristol held majority stake
Acquired by Francois Pinault (Groupe Artemis); major cellar and vineyard renovation begins
Withdrew from en primeur system; wines now released at the estate's chosen maturation date

wines.description: The most mineral and austere Pauillac first-growth, famous for decades-long development in bottle. Cabernet Sauvignon from the Enclos parcel produces extraordinary precision; withdrew from en primeur in 2012.

wines.name: Les Forts de Latour
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 50-60% new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Second wine of Latour from peripheral L'Enclos plots and younger vines, with a higher Merlot proportion than the grand vin. Retains Latour's mineral iron signature at a fraction of the price.

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with Bordelaise sauce
pairings.why: Classic Left Bank pairing; the wine's firm tannins and cassis core partner with lamb's sweetness and the sauce's bone marrow richness.
Muscle-driven tannins from Cabernet cut the fat of a well-marbled ribeye; garlic's mellow sweetness after roasting bridges the fruit.
Game's iron-tinged flavour aligns with Les Forts' characteristic mineral and tobacco notes.

history.summary: Les Forts de Latour was created in 1966 to use fruit from peripheral parcels and younger vines, while maintaining the same rigorous winemaking standards as the grand vin. Named after the medieval forts (fortified farmhouses) that historically guarded Pauillac's northern border. Aged with the same care as the grand vin but with fewer new barrels.

milestones.event: First vintage of Les Forts de Latour produced
Like the grand vin, now released at producer's chosen maturity rather than en primeur

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Latour since 1966, sourced from peripheral Enclos plots and younger vines. Higher Merlot proportion softens Latour's austere structure while retaining its mineral iron signature.

wines.name: Le Pauillac de Chateau Latour
wines.oak_regime: 20% new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Third wine of Latour produced since 1989. Merlot-dominant and made primarily from young vines; developed for the restaurant market as a generously fruity, earlier-drinking Pauillac.

pairings.why: Lighter-bodied than the grand vin, Le Pauillac's fruit-forward character pairs well with everyday roasted poultry.
Accessible tannins and fresh fruit do not overwhelm delicate cured meats or pate.
Classic Pauillac pairing at a more accessible format; medium structure and cassis notes work well with quick-grilled lamb.
