history.summary: Created annually since 1989 for the restaurant trade, Le Pauillac de Chateau Latour is made from the estate's youngest vines and parcels not meeting the selection criteria for Les Forts de Latour. It was produced occasionally since 1965 but became a consistent annual release in 1989.

milestones.event: First consistent annual release of Le Pauillac de Chateau Latour

wines.description: Third wine of Chateau Latour since 1989, produced annually for the restaurant market. Merlot-dominant, generously fruity, and intended for earlier drinking than the estate's two senior labels.

wines.name: La Réserve de Léoville Barton
wines.oak_regime: 16-18 months in French oak barrels, 60% new

taste.summary: An elegant and approachable expression of Saint-Julien from younger vines, with a higher Merlot proportion giving early accessibility and soft ripe fruit.

pairings.why: The wine's supple tannins and ripe fruit complement the mild richness of roasted poultry.
Fruit-forward approachability and gentle grip make this a natural match for a well-made burger.

pairings.dish: Grilled pork tenderloin with cherry sauce
pairings.why: Sweet cherry echoes the wine's red-fruit profile; pork fat is balanced by fresh acidity.

history.summary: The second wine of Château Léoville Barton, sourced from younger vines and plots not selected for the grand vin. A higher proportion of Merlot than the grand vin ensures more immediate drinkability while retaining the hallmark Barton elegance.

milestones.event: La Réserve de Léoville Barton introduced as the estate's second wine

wines.description: Second wine of Château Léoville Barton, offering the Saint-Julien character of the estate at an accessible level. Higher Merlot content than the grand vin ensures approachability in youth.

wines.name: Château Léoville Barton
wines.oak_regime: Approximately 50% new French oak barrels, 20 months

taste.summary: Timeless Saint-Julien classic: a Cabernet-dominant blend with classic Médoc pencil shavings, licorice and cassis, built for patience with rich powdery tannins.

pairings.why: The wine's structured tannins and cassis fruit are a textbook match for the minerality of slow-roasted lamb.

pairings.dish: Braised short rib with sauce bordelaise
pairings.why: Long braising softens the beef to mirror the wine's velvety tannins; the bone marrow sauce echoes cedar notes.

pairings.dish: Wild duck breast with blackcurrant reduction
pairings.why: Game bird's richness and the sauce's acidity dovetail with the wine's dark fruit and firm structure.
Aged cheese sharpens the wine's fruit and integrates its oak spice.

history.summary: Hugh Barton purchased Léoville Barton in 1826 as part of the historic Léoville estate division. The Barton family, originally Irish négociants, have maintained unbroken ownership for nearly 200 years, vinifying at the adjacent Château Langoa Barton using traditional wooden fermentation vats dating to 1963.

milestones.event: Hugh Barton acquires the property from the divided Léoville estate
Classified as Deuxième Cru in the 1855 Bordeaux Classification
Anthony Barton takes sole charge; begins long quality-improvement era
Anthony Barton passes; Lilian Barton Sartorius and children continue stewardship

wines.description: Barton family-owned since 1826, this Saint-Julien Deuxième Cru is one of Bordeaux's most consistent values among classified growths: classically structured Cabernet blends built for long ageing.

wines.name: Clos du Marquis
wines.oak_regime: French oak barrels, approximately 60% new, 18 months

taste.summary: A cuvée in its own right rather than a simple second wine: structured Saint-Julien Cabernet with its own distinct terroir personality from western parcels of the estate, offering outstanding value relative to the grand vin.

pairings.dish: Roasted duck with cherry and herb reduction
pairings.why: Fruit richness and acidity of the wine elevate the sweetness of duck with its cherry glaze.
Classic Médoc pairing where Cabernet structure meets the bone marrow richness of a Bordelaise.
The wine's firm tannins and dark herb register work beautifully with slow-cooked beef stew.

history.summary: First produced in 1902, Clos du Marquis was long mistaken for the estate's second wine but is officially recognised as a distinct cuvée from specific western parcels of the Léoville-Las Cases domain. It embodies the Saint-Julien appellation character in its own right.

milestones.event: Clos du Marquis first produced as a distinct bottling from western estate parcels
Le Petit Lion du Marquis de Las Cases introduced as the true second wine, clarifying Clos du Marquis's separate identity

wines.description: A distinct cuvée (not a second wine) of Château Léoville-Las Cases, produced since 1902 from western parcels of the estate. Excellent value for structured, age-worthy Saint-Julien Cabernet.

wines.name: Grand Vin de Léoville du Marquis de Las Cases
wines.oak_regime: Approximately 90% new French oak barrels, 18 months

taste.summary: One of the great super-seconds of Bordeaux: extraordinarily concentrated cassis fruit with almost first-growth precision, dense tannins demanding patient cellaring of 15 or more years.

pairings.dish: Saddle of venison with black pepper sauce
pairings.why: The wine's massive tannin structure and dark fruit demand the intensity of aged game with a peppery reduction.
Lamb's mineral character and earthy truffle are transformed by the wine's concentrated black fruit and cigar-box cedar.

pairings.dish: Aged Mimolette or mature Comté (36 months)
pairings.why: Crystalline, nutty aged cheese cuts through the wine's dense tannin and highlights its violet florality.
Extraordinary marbling requires the extraordinary tannin grip of a great Las Cases vintage.

history.summary: Léoville-Las Cases descends from the original Léoville estate divided after the French Revolution. The Delon family has owned it since the late 19th century, producing consistently first-growth quality from the walled Grand Clos adjoining Château Latour. The 2022 vintage received 100 points from both Wine Advocate and James Suckling.

milestones.event: Classified as Deuxième Cru in the 1855 Bordeaux Classification
Grand Vin label with 'Grand Clos' designation formalised
Le Petit Lion du Marquis de Las Cases introduced as official second wine
Vintage receives 100 points from Wine Advocate and James Suckling

wines.description: The flagship wine of Château Léoville-Las Cases, sourced from the historic Grand Clos adjoining Château Latour. Delon family-owned, Cabernet-dominant, consistently performing at first-growth level and one of the most age-worthy wines in the Médoc.

wines.name: Le Petit Lion du Marquis de Las Cases
wines.oak_regime: French oak barrels, proportion new oak lower than grand vin

taste.summary: The official second wine of Léoville-Las Cases since 2007, Merlot-dominant for earlier accessibility while retaining the Saint-Julien precision of the parent estate.

pairings.why: Merlot-driven softness and ripe fruit pair elegantly with the mild richness of herb-roasted chicken.
Spiced ground lamb finds a complementary partner in the wine's violet florality and soft tannins.
The wine's supple fruit and gentle grip pair versatilely with a range of cured meats and liver pâtés.

history.summary: Created in 2007 as the official second wine of Léoville-Las Cases to clarify the status of Clos du Marquis as its own distinct cuvée. The higher Merlot content ensures approachability in youth while maintaining the estate's signature precision.

milestones.event: First vintage of Le Petit Lion du Marquis de Las Cases as the estate's official second wine

wines.description: Official second wine of Château Léoville-Las Cases since 2007. Merlot-dominant for approachability while retaining Saint-Julien elegance and the estate's characteristic precision.

wines.name: Blanc de Lynch-Bages
wines.oak_regime: 50% new French oak, 20% one-year-old barrels, 30% tank vinification

taste.summary: Historically one of the first white wines produced by a Grand Cru Classe in the Medoc. Primarily Sauvignon Blanc driven with Semillon body; partially barrel-fermented but retains freshness through tank-vinified component.

pairings.why: Classic white Bordeaux pairing; citrus and Sauvignon Blanc freshness cuts the browned butter without competing.
The wine's high acidity and citrus notes balance the richness of butter while Muscadelle adds floral lift to shellfish.
Sauvignon Blanc's grassy acidity is the classic partner to fresh chevre; Semillon adds creamy weight.

history.summary: Blanc de Lynch-Bages was first produced with the 1990 vintage, making it one of the pioneering white wine efforts among the Medoc Grand Cru Classe estates. The white vines are planted on distinct sandy parcels separate from the main Cabernet-dominant vineyards. It was voted Wine Spectator Top 100 #67 in its debut year.

milestones.event: First vintage; one of the first white wines from a Medoc Grand Cru Classe estate
Wine Spectator Top 100 #67 for the debut vintage

wines.description: White wine of Lynch-Bages since 1990, historically one of the first whites from a Medoc Grand Cru Classe. Sauvignon Blanc-dominant with Semillon and Muscadelle; partially barrel-fermented for complexity.

wines.name: Echo de Lynch-Bages
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in 35% new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Second wine of Lynch-Bages since 2007 (previously sold as Chateau Haut-Bages Averous). Higher Merlot component than the grand vin provides accessible fruit and earlier drinking; retains characteristic Lynch-Bages tobacco and cassis signature.

pairings.why: An accessible bistro pairing; the wine's cassis and soft earth complement steak without needing the full structure of the grand vin.
Echo's fresher, lighter profile handles spice better than the full-bodied grand vin; the wine's cassis cools the heat.
Earthiness in the wine echoes the mushroom umami; medium body does not overwhelm a risotto.

history.summary: The Lynch-Bages second wine was sold for decades as Chateau Haut-Bages Averous. In 2007, it was rebranded Echo de Lynch-Bages for easier pronunciation and clearer branding as the estate's second label. The wine draws on fruit from younger vines and Merlot-dominant parcels.

milestones.event: Rebranded from Chateau Haut-Bages Averous to Echo de Lynch-Bages

wines.description: Second wine of Lynch-Bages since 2007 (formerly Chateau Haut-Bages Averous). More Merlot-rich and earlier-drinking than the grand vin; retains the estate's cassis and tobacco character at an accessible price.

wines.name: Chateau Lynch-Bages
wines.classification: Pauillac AOC, Cinquieme Cru Classe (1855 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 12 to 15 months in 70-75% new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Lynch-Bages consistently overperforms its Fifth Growth classification. A richer, more extrovert Pauillac than the first-growths, with cassis generosity, smoky tobacco, and a robust structure that ages beautifully over 20-30 years.

pairings.why: The classic Bordeaux pairing; Lynch-Bages' cassis fruit and tobacco echo the earthy marrow in the Bordelaise sauce.
Richer than many Pauillacs at this classification, Lynch-Bages' dark fruit and firm structure handle the fat of pork belly well.
Game charcuterie amplifies the wine's tobacoo and earthy register while the acidic cornichons cleanse the palate.
Firm French mountain cheese pulls out Lynch-Bages' graphite and cedar complexity.

history.summary: Named after Thomas Lynch, an Irish immigrant who married into the Drouillard family and acquired the Bages plateau in the early 18th century. Classified as Fifth Growth in 1855. The Cazes family has owned it since 1939 under Jean-Michel Cazes and now Jean-Charles Cazes. Despite Fifth Growth status, Lynch-Bages is considered a 'super fifth' regularly trading at Second-Growth prices.

milestones.event: Classified Cinquieme Cru Classe in the Medoc classification
Acquired by the Cazes family; Jean-Michel Cazes later elevated its profile globally
Wine Spectator Top 100 #3 for the 2020 vintage; establishes Lynch-Bages as a benchmark fifth growth

wines.description: Pauillac Fifth Growth that consistently overperforms its 1855 classification. Rich cassis, smoky tobacco, and robust structure make it one of the most dependable and characterful wines in the Medoc.

wines.name: Château Margaux
wines.oak_regime: 100% new French oak barrels, 18-24 months

taste.summary: The most perfumed of the Médoc first growths: haunting floral aromatics of roses and violets above a core of concentrated cassis, extraordinary saline minerality, and tannins demanding decades of patience.

pairings.why: The iconic Bordeaux pairing: structured first-growth tannins and mineral precision meet the finest regional lamb.
Game birds' gamey intensity and earthy character demand the complexity and aromatic depth of a great Margaux.
Earthy truffle and the wine's graphite-and-violet precision create an extraordinary aromatic dialogue.
The wine's grippy tannins and high acidity cut through the richness of mi-cuit foie gras.

history.summary: One of the world's most iconic wine names, documented since the 12th century as 'La Mothe de Margaux' and restructured by the Lestonnac family from 1572. The Mentzelopoulos family acquired the estate in 1977 and led its modern renaissance under director Paul Pontallier (1983-2016), followed by Philippe Bascaules.

milestones.event: Lestonnac family restructures the property entirely to viticulture
Classified as Premier Cru in the Bordeaux 1855 Classification
André Mentzelopoulos purchases the estate and begins quality revival
Paul Pontallier appointed director, leading 33-year quality era
Vintage achieves 100 points from Wine Advocate (William Kelley)

wines.description: Bordeaux's most perfumed Premier Cru, owned by the Mentzelopoulos family since 1977. Cabernet Sauvignon-dominant blends from the Margaux AOC's famed deep gravel soils, celebrated for their floral aromatics, silky texture, and extraordinary longevity.

wines.name: Pavillon Blanc du Château Margaux
wines.oak_regime: 33% new French oak barrels, 7-8 months

taste.summary: A rare Sauvignon Blanc white Bordeaux of extraordinary mineral precision: gunsmoke, slate and saline drive a taut citrus frame that ages beautifully over a decade or more.

pairings.why: The wine's saline minerality and racy acidity are a sublime match for the brininess of Atlantic oysters.

pairings.dish: Grilled Dover sole with beurre blanc
pairings.why: Delicate white fish and lemony butter sauce amplify the wine's citrus and mineral precision.
Sweet crustacean richness is cut by the wine's racy acidity and gunsmoke minerality.
Fresh chèvre's tangy creaminess is a natural partner for the wine's Sauvignon florality and acidity.

history.summary: Pavillon Blanc is produced from 12 hectares of Sauvignon Blanc planted on the Margaux estate; uniquely, it cannot carry the Margaux AOC because Sauvignon Blanc is not permitted in the appellation, so it is sold as Bordeaux AOC. First produced in 1921, it is among the most sought-after white Bordeaux wines.

milestones.event: First vintage of Pavillon Blanc du Château Margaux produced
Pavillon Blanc Second Vin introduced for the 2022 vintage

wines.description: 100% Sauvignon Blanc white Bordeaux from a 12-hectare plot on the Château Margaux estate. Cannot carry the Margaux AOC as Sauvignon Blanc is not permitted in the appellation; sold as Bordeaux AOC. One of France's greatest white wines.

wines.name: Pavillon Rouge du Château Margaux
wines.oak_regime: New French oak barrels, 18 months

taste.summary: Exquisite second wine of the most famous first growth, sharing the same subtle power and floral finesse as the grand vin while offering earlier accessibility.

pairings.dish: Magret de canard with cherry compote
pairings.why: Duck breast's richness and the sweet cherry reduction echo the wine's rose-petal florality and ripe cassis.

pairings.dish: Roast lamb rack with garlic and rosemary
pairings.why: The wine's Margaux-characteristic floral tannins complement herb-crusted lamb's mineral sweetness.
Earthy umami of mushroom and truffle find resonance in the wine's cedar-spice register.
Crystalline nutty cheese sharpens Margaux's floral fruit and integrates the oak spice.

history.summary: The second wine concept at Château Margaux dates to the early 17th century, but Pavillon Rouge took its permanent name in 1908 and was relaunched in its current form in 1977 when the Mentzelopoulos family revived the estate. Around 30% of the harvest is allocated to Pavillon Rouge.

milestones.event: Name 'Pavillon Rouge du Château Margaux' formally adopted
Pavillon Rouge relaunched alongside the estate revival under Mentzelopoulos

wines.description: The storied second wine of Château Margaux, named since 1908. Made from around 30% of the Margaux harvest and sharing the grand vin's floral Margaux personality at a more accessible price.

wines.name: Chateau Meyney
wines.oak_regime: 18 to 24 months in 50% new French oak barriques

taste.finish: long, structured with spice and dark fruit
taste.summary: One of Saint-Estephe's most consistent and value-oriented estates, Meyney produces full-bodied, tannic wines with a distinctive spice and dark berry character. Notable for its high Petit Verdot content (10%) contributing structure and aromatic complexity.

pairings.why: Meyney's high Petit Verdot content adds spice that mirrors the peppercorn sauce; robust tannins and dark fruit cut the beef's richness.

pairings.dish: Cassoulet with duck confit and Toulouse sausage
pairings.why: The wine's firm structure and dark fruit work with the richness of slow-cooked cassoulet; spice notes echo the sausage seasoning.
Hard French mountain cheese pulls out Meyney's spice and dark fruit structure beautifully.

history.summary: One of the oldest estates in Saint-Estephe, Meyney was known as the Prieuré des Couleys in the 17th century. Classified as Cru Bourgeois since the early 20th century, it withdrew from the Cru Bourgeois label in 2008 due to classification restrictions. Currently owned by Credit Agricole Grands Crus, the estate is one of the most reliable Saint-Estephe names at any price.

milestones.event: Estate recorded as Prieure des Couleys in historical documents
Voluntarily left the Cru Bourgeois classification system

wines.description: Historic Saint-Estephe estate with roots to 1666; one of the appellation's most consistent value wines. Full-bodied with 10% Petit Verdot adding distinctive spice; 18 to 24 months in oak.

wines.name: Prieur de Meyney
wines.oak_regime: 16 months in 10-15% new French oak barriques

taste.finish: medium with fruit and soft structure
taste.summary: Second wine of Chateau Meyney since 1979, named for the estate's 17th-century priory origins. Produced from younger vines (approximately 35-40 years) using minimal new oak to preserve fresh fruit character; a reliable everyday Saint-Estephe.

pairings.dish: Roasted chicken legs with garlic and lemon
pairings.why: Approachable tannins and fresh fruit make Prieur de Meyney a natural match for simple roasted poultry.
The wine's medium body and fresh acidity cut through hearty peasant stews without needing the grand vin's full structure.
Gentle tannins and red fruit do not overwhelm the delicate balance of preserved meats; acid cuts the fat.

history.summary: Prieur de Meyney takes its name from the estate's original 17th-century designation as Prieure des Couleys. Produced since 1979 from younger vines and supple-producing parcels with lower oak influence to preserve freshness. The same biologically rigorous approach as the grand vin applies.

milestones.event: First vintage of Prieur de Meyney

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Meyney since 1979, named for the estate's priory heritage. Produced from younger vines with minimal new oak; a reliable and affordable Saint-Estephe.

wines.name: Chateau Haut-Lariveau
wines.oak_regime: 15 months in French oak, moderate new wood proportion

taste.summary: 100% Merlot Fronsac from the 4.5-hectare Haut-Lariveau parcel acquired by the Hubau family in 1990. More approachable and fruit-forward than its Canon-Fronsac sibling, farmed with the same biodynamic discipline.

pairings.why: Soft Merlot tannins and blackcurrant fruit complement mild poultry; herbal tarragon echoes the fresh tobacco note.

pairings.dish: Pasta with rich tomato and beef ragu
pairings.why: Medium-body and plum fruit work with a long-simmered meat sauce; fresh acidity keeps the wine lively.
Pepper and spice in both the sausage and the wine create a coherent harmony.

history.summary: Chateau Haut-Lariveau was purchased by the Hubau family in 1990, two years after they acquired Moulin Pey-Labrie. The 4.5-hectare parcel is planted entirely to Merlot and produces the family's entry-level Fronsac, farmed organically under the same Ecocert certification as the Canon-Fronsac estate.

milestones.event: Hubau family acquires the 4.5-ha Haut-Lariveau parcel in Fronsac

wines.description: 100% Merlot Fronsac from the Hubau family's 4.5-hectare Haut-Lariveau parcel, acquired 1990. Fruit-forward and approachable; farmed biodynamically under the same Ecocert certification as Moulin Pey-Labrie.

wines.name: Chateau Moulin Pey-Labrie
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 33% new French oak barrels; malolactic fermentation in barrel

taste.summary: The benchmark Canon-Fronsac red from 6.75 hectares of 65-year-average-age vines, farmed biodynamically and organically. Merlot with 5% Malbec produces a fragrant, perfumed wine with remarkable freshness for the Right Bank.

pairings.why: The wine's bright cherry and fresh acidity complement the mild gaminess of rabbit; herbs echo the lavender note.

pairings.dish: Roasted guinea fowl with wild mushrooms
pairings.why: Forest floor and earthy mushroom flavours in the wine match the rustic richness of the bird.
Merlot fruit holds up to slow-cooked lamb; the wine's bright acidity lifts the richness of the gratin.
A classic bistro red-wine braise that matches the wine's aromatic freshness and round tannins.

history.summary: Gregoire and Chantal Hubau, a chemist and computer scientist by training, purchased the estate in 1988 with no prior winemaking background and engaged Michel Rolland as consultant. They adopted 100% biodynamic farming, which resulted in Ecocert organic certification. The estate now encompasses 6.75 hectares of Canon-Fronsac plus the separately labelled Chateau Haut-Lariveau (Fronsac) and Chateau des Combes Canon (Canon-Fronsac).

milestones.event: Hubau family acquires estate; Michel Rolland engaged as consultant
Chateau Haut-Lariveau parcel acquired
Ecocert organic certification obtained

wines.description: Canon-Fronsac benchmark from 6.75 ha of biodynamic Merlot-Malbec vines averaging 65 years. Fragrant and perfumed with high acidity; Ecocert-certified organic; Michel Rolland consulting since founding.

wines.name: Aile d'Argent
wines.oak_regime: 50% new French oak barriques, 20% partial malolactic fermentation

taste.finish: long with citrus and mineral lift
taste.summary: White wine of Mouton Rothschild from 7 hectares of sandy-gravelly soils, reviving a historic Medoc tradition. Rich and complex; Sauvignon Blanc provides drive while Semillon adds texture; partial barrel fermentation contributes subtle vanilla without overwhelming fruit.

pairings.why: The wine's citrus acidity cuts the richness of beurre blanc while its Semillon texture pairs with the firm white fish.
Luxurious shellfish calls for a white wine with weight; Aile d'Argent's Semillon component supplies it without losing freshness.
Sauvignon Blanc's bright grassiness is the classic pairing with chevres; Aile d'Argent elevates the formula with barrel complexity.

history.summary: Philippine de Rothschild initiated white wine plantings in the early 1980s, reviving a tradition of producing white Bordeaux in the Medoc that had nearly disappeared. The first vintage was 1991. Aile d'Argent is one of the benchmark white wines of the Left Bank, produced in limited quantity from 7 hectares of sandy-gravelly soils.

milestones.event: First vintage of Aile d'Argent, reviving historic white Bordeaux tradition in the Medoc

wines.description: White wine of Mouton Rothschild since 1991, from 7 hectares of sandy-gravelly Pauillac soils. Sauvignon Blanc dominant blend with Semillon and barrel fermentation; revives a historic Left Bank white tradition.

wines.name: Le Petit Mouton de Mouton Rothschild
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 50% new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Made specifically from younger vines harvested earlier to preserve freshness. Le Petit Mouton shows a younger, more approachable face of the Mouton house style: cassis-driven, with cedar and gentle tobacco, earlier-drinking but still structured.

pairings.why: Earthy herbs and slow-cooked lamb complement Le Petit Mouton's cedar and cassis character at an accessible price point.
The wine's dark cherry and cassis notes echo the fruit reduction; duck fat is balanced by firm Cabernet tannins.
Nutty hard cheese draws out Le Petit Mouton's tobacco and cedar subtlety.

history.summary: Le Petit Mouton was introduced with the 1994 vintage as a second wine bottled under the Mouton Rothschild estate. Unlike many second wines, it is made specifically from younger-vine fruit harvested at a separate, earlier date chosen to retain freshness and aromatic lift rather than simply from declassified grand vin lots.

milestones.event: First vintage of Le Petit Mouton de Mouton Rothschild

wines.description: Second wine of Mouton Rothschild since 1994, made from younger vines harvested earlier for freshness. Cassis and cedar character of the house style in a more approachable format.

wines.name: Chateau Mouton Rothschild
wines.classification: Pauillac AOC, Premier Cru Classe (elevated from Deuxieme in 1973)
wines.oak_regime: 18 to 22 months in 100% new French oak barriques

taste.finish: very long with charcoal and dark berry
taste.summary: The most opulent of the Pauillac first-growths; Mouton Rothschild emphasises blackcurrant concentration, dark chocolate, and an extrovert aromatic generosity that sets it apart from the pencil-mineral restraint of Lafite and Latour.

pairings.why: Mouton's powerful dark fruit and ripe tannins match the extraordinary fat content of Wagyu; chocolate tones find an echo in beef's natural glutamates.

pairings.dish: Roasted rack of lamb with black truffle
pairings.why: Truffle and dark fruit in the wine mirror the aromatic complexity of truffle-studded lamb.
The wine's cassis-and-licorice register mirrors the fruit in the sauce; firm tannins cut game's gaminess.
Crystalline aged cheese amplifies Mouton's fruit and tobacco while walnuts echo bitter chocolate notes.

history.summary: Acquired by Baron Nathaniel de Rothschild in 1853. For over a century classified as a Second Growth, Baron Philippe de Rothschild campaigned for reclassification. In 1973, Mouton Rothschild became the only wine ever to be upgraded in the 1855 classification, from Second to First Growth. Since 1945, each vintage has featured a different label artwork by a renowned artist -- a tradition initiated by Philippe de Rothschild.

milestones.event: Acquired by Baron Nathaniel de Rothschild at auction
First artist label commissioned: 'V pour Victoire' by Philippe Julian to mark the Allied victory
Elevated to Premier Cru Classe in the only official amendment to the 1855 classification
Jean-Emmanuel Danjoy appointed technical director; modernisation of winemaking approach

wines.description: Pauillac first-growth elevated from second in 1973; only wine ever reclassified upward in the 1855 system. Famous for annual artist-label series since 1945 and extrovert dark-fruit opulence.

wines.name: Alter Ego de Palmer
wines.oak_regime: 25-40% new French oak barrels, 16-18 months

taste.summary: Not a second wine but a distinct cuvée emphasising freshness and fruit intensity over the grand vin's power: Margaux character with more immediate expression, distinguished by its energy and precision.

pairings.why: Alter Ego's freshness and fruit intensity cut cleanly through the richness of a peppercorn-sauced steak.
Duck fat's richness and the wine's lively fruit create a classic combination.
Herb-scented slow-cooked lamb is mirrored in Alter Ego's aromatic freshness and violet notes.

history.summary: Introduced in 1998, Alter Ego de Palmer was conceived not as a second wine but as a distinct cuvée with its own identity, emphasising freshness of fruit and intensity over the structural ambition of the grand vin. Around 40% of Palmer's production is sold as Alter Ego. It shares the estate's Demeter biodynamic certification.

milestones.event: Alter Ego de Palmer introduced as a distinct cuvée, not a second wine

wines.description: A distinct cuvée (not a second wine) of Château Palmer, first made in 1998. Emphasises freshness and fruit intensity; Demeter-certified biodynamic since the 2018 vintage. Approximately 40% of Palmer's production.

verified.address_quoted: ChÂteau Palmer lieu-dit issan 33460 Margaux-Cantenac France

wines.name: Château Palmer
wines.oak_regime: 50-70% new French oak barrels and foudres, 20-22 months combined

taste.summary: Classified Third Growth performing at first-growth level: extraordinary richness from an unusually high Merlot proportion, with Thomas Duroux's biodynamic conversion delivering added aromatic complexity and freshness.

pairings.dish: Roasted Pauillac lamb with herb crust
pairings.why: The wine's dense Merlot fruit and violet florality complement the mineral sweetness of regional milk-fed lamb.

pairings.dish: Rack of venison with black truffle
pairings.why: The wine's concentrated dark fruit and biodynamic earthy depth are amplified by truffle and gamey venison.

pairings.dish: Slow-braised short rib with mushroom duxelles
pairings.why: Merlot richness and fine tannins meet the umami intensity of braised beef and mushroom.
Crystalline hard cheese sharpens the wine's dense fruit and integrates its mineral oak.

history.summary: Named after Major General Charles Palmer who acquired the property in 1814, the estate passed through various owners before the current Société Civile ownership (Mahler-Besse and Sichel families). Winemaker Thomas Duroux's arrival in 2004 drove a complete biodynamic conversion completed by 2014 and Demeter-certified from the 2018 vintage.

milestones.event: Major General Charles Palmer acquires the property and names it
Classified as Troisième Cru in the 1855 Bordeaux Classification
Alter Ego de Palmer introduced as a distinct second cuvée
Thomas Duroux appointed technical director, begins biodynamic conversion
Demeter biodynamic certification achieved for all Palmer wines

wines.description: Margaux Troisième Cru consistently performing at first-growth level. Demeter-certified biodynamic since the 2018 vintage, with an unusually high Merlot proportion giving a lush, opulent style. One of Bordeaux's most sought-after wines.

verified.address_quoted: ChÂteau Palmer lieu-dit issan 33460 Margaux-Cantenac France

wines.name: Le Clementin du Pape Clement Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Approximately 30-40% new French oak; 12-14 months

taste.summary: The second red wine of Pape Clement since 1986. Merlot-dominant for approachability; round red cherry and currant fruit with cedar and soft spice notes. Designed for earlier drinking than the grand vin while reflecting the same Pessac terroir.

pairings.why: Medium body and cherry fruit are classic bistro partners for simply cooked steak; tannins and acidity cut through the fat.
Earthiness of lentils and savory pork resonate with the wine's soft tannin and herbal notes.
Medium-bodied red at a comfortable temperature for everyday chicken; balanced fruit won't dominate the dish.

history.summary: Le Clementin du Pape Clement was created in 1986 as the second wine of the estate, intended to offer the pleasure of Pape Clement's Pessac-Leognan terroir in an approachable, earlier-drinking style. The name acknowledges Pope Clement V, the estate's historical patron. It is sold at a price point accessible to everyday drinkers while sharing the same estate management and care.

milestones.event: Le Clementin introduced as the second wine of Chateau Pape Clement

wines.description: Second red wine of Chateau Pape Clement, produced since 1986. Merlot-dominant blend from the same Pessac-Leognan estate as the grand vin; approachable dark cherry and plum fruit for earlier drinking.

verified.address_quoted: 216 avenue du Docteur Nancel Penard, 33600 Pessac, France

wines.name: Chateau Pape Clement Blanc
wines.classification: Cru Classe de Graves (white); Pessac-Leognan AOC
wines.oak_regime: 100% new French oak; 12 months on lees; barrel-fermented

taste.summary: A classified Graves white from one of Bordeaux's most historic estates. Sauvignon Blanc leads with fresh pink grapefruit and lime blossom; Semillon adds body and Sauvignon Gris a distinctive floral note. 100% new oak fermentation produces a rich, mineral, long-lived dry white.

pairings.why: The wine's mineral salinity and citrus precision are the archetypal complement to briny Bordeaux oysters.
Herbal fennel and delicate fish amplify the wine's citrus and herbal notes; high acidity cleanses the palate.
Sauvignon Blanc's classic partnership with goat's cheese; honey mirrors the wine's hint of roundness from Semillon.
Sweet crustacean and herbed butter find a perfect counterpoint in grapefruit acidity and white flower aromatics.

history.summary: White wine production at Pape Clement was significantly expanded and elevated under Bernard Magrez from 1985 onward. The white grand vin is a classified Graves white recognised since the 1959 Graves classification update. Production tripled after Magrez's investment in white-wine winemaking equipment.

milestones.event: Pape Clement Blanc recognised in the Graves white wine classification
Bernard Magrez acquires estate; white wine winemaking investment begins

wines.description: Classified Graves white from Bordeaux's oldest estate. Barrel-fermented blend of Sauvignon Blanc, Semillon, and Sauvignon Gris; fresh pink grapefruit, mineral precision, and a long saline finish.

verified.address_quoted: 216 avenue du Docteur Nancel Penard, 33600 Pessac, France

wines.name: Chateau Pape Clement
wines.oak_regime: Principally French oak barriques, 40% new oak with amphora and neutral vessel component from 2023; 18 months

taste.summary: From the oldest wine estate in Bordeaux, tracing its history to the 14th-century papacy. Cabernet-and-Merlot blend delivering cassis, graphite, and tobacco with flawless tannins; in great vintages approaches first-growth complexity.

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with Bordeaux reduction
pairings.why: A classic Pessac-Leognan pairing; the cassis and scorched-earth character mirror the reduction while tannins harmonise with lamb.
Graphite and lead-pencil notes in the wine echo truffle earthiness; full body suits the richness of tenderloin.
Game and dark spice in the wine complement the gamey depth of wild boar; tannins cut through fat.
Salty, piquant blue cheese amplifies the wine's dark fruit while the walnut bread adds textural contrast.

history.summary: The oldest wine property in Bordeaux, Pape Clement takes its name from Bertrand de Got, Archbishop of Bordeaux who became Pope Clement V in 1305 and who planted vines at the Pessac estate. The property passed through various clerical and secular hands before arriving with Bernard Magrez in 1985, who dramatically elevated quality through investment in viticulture and winemaking.

milestones.event: First documented vines at the Pessac property under the Archbishop of Bordeaux
Bertrand de Got becomes Pope Clement V; property renamed Pape Clement
Classified as Cru Classe de Graves in the Graves classification
Bernard Magrez acquires the estate and begins quality-focused transformation

wines.description: Bordeaux's oldest estate, tracing its roots to Pope Clement V in 1305. Cru Classe de Graves red blending Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot from a 33-hectare Pessac vineyard; dense cassis, graphite, and tobacco with outstanding ageing potential.

verified.address_quoted: 216 avenue du Docteur Nancel Penard, 33600 Pessac, France

wines.name: Aromes de Pavie
wines.oak_regime: 18-24 months in 50% new French oak barrels; from dedicated 11-hectare parcel since 2018

taste.summary: Originally the second wine of Pavie, now an independent brand since 2018 sourced from a dedicated 11-hectare east-plateau parcel. Merlot-Cabernet Franc blend of medium body with dark cherry, espresso and salty-mineral character; approachable on release.

pairings.why: Medium-weight fruit and espresso notes match grilled lamb elegantly without the grand vin's power.
Dark cherry fruit in the wine mirrors the sauce; soft tannins integrate with duck fat.
Bright acidity cuts pork fat; mineral finish lifts cured meat flavors.

history.summary: Aromes de Pavie was created in 2005, replacing the former second wine Chateau Tour Simard. Produced initially from younger vines across the estate, from 2018 it has been repositioned as an independent brand with its own 11-hectare parcel of east-plateau vines averaging 53 years in age, with a dedicated separate cellar. The shift reflects Perse's commitment to terroir specificity.

milestones.event: First vintage of Aromes de Pavie, replacing Chateau Tour Simard
Repositioned as standalone brand with dedicated 11-hectare east-plateau parcel

wines.description: Formerly the second wine of Chateau Pavie, now an independent brand from a dedicated 11-hectare east-plateau parcel. Merlot-Cabernet Franc blend with dark cherry, espresso and salty-mineral character; approachable on release.

wines.name: Chateau Pavie
wines.classification: Saint-Emilion Premier Grand Cru Classe A (2012 and 2022 classifications)
wines.oak_regime: 18-24 months in 75% new French oak barrels

taste.summary: From 42 hectares at Saint-Emilion's highest point (110m), Pavie delivers concentrated, structured Merlot-dominant blends with extraordinary mineral depth. Pairs elegance with considerable power; requires minimum 15 years to fully integrate.

pairings.dish: Roasted rack of lamb with truffle potato gratin
pairings.why: Powerful tannin structure integrates with lamb's collagen-rich rack; truffle echoes the wine's earthy mineral register.

pairings.dish: Beef entrecote with bone marrow butter
pairings.why: Full-body and concentrated fruit match the richness of prime beef and marrow; acidity cleanses.

pairings.dish: Wild mushroom risotto with aged Parmigiano
pairings.why: Earthy mushroom and umami cheese amplify the wine's truffle and mineral dimension.
The wine's concentration and dark fruit intensity stand up to the richness of foie gras.

history.summary: Chateau Pavie was purchased by Gerard Perse in 1998 for 31 million euros, after which it underwent a dramatic quality transformation. Previously classified as Premier Grand Cru Classe B since 1954, Pavie was promoted to the top rank (A) in the 2012 reclassification alongside Angelus. The 2022 reclassification confirmed Pavie's continued status as Premier Grand Cru Classe A, one of four estates at that level alongside the newly promoted Figeac. Gerard Perse passed away in July 2025; the estate continues under son-in-law Henrique da Costa.

milestones.event: First classified as Premier Grand Cru Classe B
Gerard Perse purchases the estate; quality transformation begins
Promoted to Premier Grand Cru Classe A in Saint-Emilion reclassification
Status as Premier Grand Cru Classe A confirmed in latest classification

wines.description: Grand vin from Chateau Pavie, a Saint-Emilion Premier Grand Cru Classe A estate. From 42 hectares at the appellation's highest elevation, a full-bodied, concentrated Merlot-dominant blend with mineral depth and remarkable ageing potential.

wines.name: La Chapelle de La Mission Haut-Brion
wines.oak_regime: Percentage of new oak lower than grand vin; approximately 18 months

taste.summary: The second wine of La Mission Haut-Brion, incorporating grapes from younger vines and since 2006 from the former La Tour Haut-Brion vineyard. Shares the Talence gravel terroir; accessible dark fruit with cedar and spice, drinkable earlier than the grand vin.

pairings.why: The wine's dark cherry and spice match duck's richness; moderate tannins integrate with the cherry reduction.
Medium body and dried-herb notes complement herbed lamb without overwhelming the dish.
Cedar and dark fruit in the wine elevate a slow-cooked ragu; approachable tannin level suits a weeknight setting.

history.summary: Created at the 1991 vintage from younger vines of La Mission Haut-Brion. From 2006 onward, when La Tour Haut-Brion's vineyard was merged into La Mission, all grapes from that property were directed into La Chapelle, significantly elevating the quality of the second wine. Annual production averages approximately 4,000 cases.

milestones.event: La Chapelle de La Mission Haut-Brion introduced as the second wine
La Tour Haut-Brion vineyard integrated into La Mission; its grapes diverted entirely to La Chapelle, raising quality markedly

wines.description: Second wine of La Mission Haut-Brion, introduced in 1991 and bolstered from 2006 by the former La Tour Haut-Brion vines. Shares the estate's gravel terroir; dark fruit, cedar, and spice in an approachable medium-bodied style.

wines.name: Chateau La Mission Haut-Brion Blanc
wines.classification: Cru Classe de Graves (white); Pessac-Leognan AOC
wines.oak_regime: 100% new French oak; barrel-fermented; 13 to 16 months aging; no malolactic fermentation

taste.summary: Originally sold as Laville Haut-Brion until 2009, this is among the most complex dry whites of Bordeaux. Semillon richness and Sauvignon Blanc freshness fuse with 100% new oak to produce a wine of extraordinary mineral precision and creamy depth.

pairings.dish: Grilled Dover sole with lemon butter
pairings.why: Delicate white fish and citrus butter find partners in the wine's limey freshness and creamy mineral texture.
Sweet scallop and the nutty sweetness of cauliflower resonate with the wine's hazelnut and peach notes.

pairings.dish: Poulet de Bresse roasted with cream and tarragon
pairings.why: Cream sauce and herbal tarragon complement the wine's lanolin texture and sappy herb character.
The wine's high acidity and citrus notes cut through the oil of tempura while sweet crab flesh mirrors stone-fruit richness.

history.summary: Produced at the estate since 1927 under the name Laville Haut-Brion (named after a small adjacent property), the white wine was renamed Chateau La Mission Haut-Brion Blanc from the 2009 vintage onward when the Laville name was retired. Annual production is under 1,000 cases, making it one of the rarest Graves whites in existence.

milestones.event: White wine first produced at the estate, sold as Laville Haut-Brion
Renamed Chateau La Mission Haut-Brion Blanc; Laville Haut-Brion name retired

wines.description: One of Bordeaux's rarest dry whites, formerly Laville Haut-Brion until 2009. Semillon-dominant, barrel-fermented in 100% new oak with no malolactic fermentation; mineral, peachy, and extraordinarily long-lived.

wines.name: Chateau La Mission Haut-Brion
wines.oak_regime: 100% new French oak, approximately 22 months

taste.summary: La Mission Haut-Brion combines the gravel terroir of Talence with intense, brooding dark fruit. Blackberry, scorched earth, and graphite underpin a structured, powerful palate with velvety tannins and a finish that rivals the great first growths.

pairings.dish: Roasted rack of lamb with rosemary
pairings.why: The wine's tobacco and earthy character amplifies herbal notes; firm tannins integrate beautifully with lamb fat.
Full body and cassis depth stand up to the richness of marrow; the iodine and graphite notes complement the charred crust.
Earthy cedar and scorched-earth tones in the wine mirror truffle depth; a natural textural match.
The crystalline texture and lactic acidity of aged Cantal softens the tannin grip and lifts red-fruit notes.
Game and juniper resonate with the wine's earthy, dark-fruit and tobacco register; full body stands up to the dish.

history.summary: La Mission Haut-Brion traces its origins to 1533 when Cistercian monks cultivated vines at Talence. The modern estate was shaped between 1919 and 1983 under the Woltner family, who built its reputation as one of Pessac-Leognan's finest reds. Purchased by Domaine Clarence Dillon (owners of Haut-Brion) in 1983, it sits directly across the road from its famous neighbour and produces a wine of equal ambition if distinct character.

milestones.event: Cistercian monks at Talence cultivate the first vines on the site
Woltner family purchase the estate and begin modernising winemaking
Purchased by Domaine Clarence Dillon, owners of Chateau Haut-Brion
Vineyard of Chateau La Tour Haut-Brion integrated into La Mission; La Chapelle de La Mission becomes the single second wine

wines.description: Cru Classe de Graves from Talence, considered the closest rival to the five first growths in certain vintages. Dense blackberry, graphite, and tobacco character from 100% new oak ageing on Merlot-dominant blends.

wines.name: Les Griffons de Pichon Baron
wines.oak_regime: 60% new French oak, partially aged in amphora since 2020

taste.summary: Premium second wine of Pichon Baron introduced with the 2012 vintage. Merlot-dominant versus the Cabernet-heavy grand vin; since 2020, partially aged in amphora to add minerality and texture without excessive oak.

pairings.why: The wine's dark fruit and Merlot-sourced richness complement duck's fattiness; lentils echo earthy mineral notes.
Merlot softness and cassis fruit of Les Griffons work beautifully with lamb's sweetness and charred aubergine's smokiness.

pairings.dish: Soft-ripened cheese such as Brie de Meaux
pairings.why: Fruit-forward Merlot profile pairs with creamy soft-rinded cheeses without the tannin of the grand vin overwhelming them.

history.summary: Introduced with the 2012 vintage to replace the previous second wine Les Tourelles de Longueville as the primary second label. Les Griffons is positioned as a premium second wine rather than a declassified offcut, with selected parcels, higher Merlot, and since 2020 a portion of amphora ageing.

milestones.event: First vintage of Les Griffons de Pichon Baron; replaces Les Tourelles as premium second wine
Partial amphora ageing introduced to add mineral texture

wines.description: Premium second wine of Pichon Baron since 2012. Merlot-dominant versus the Cabernet-led grand vin; partially amphora-aged since 2020 for additional mineral texture.

wines.name: Chateau Pichon-Longueville Baron
wines.classification: Pauillac AOC, Deuxieme Cru Classe (1855 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 80% new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Pauillac second-growth with an unusually high Cabernet Sauvignon proportion for the appellation. AXA Millesimes' investment since 1987 has lifted consistency dramatically; the 2022 vintage drew especially strong critical praise in bottle.

pairings.why: Local AOC lamb is the archetypal pairing; Pichon Baron's firm Cabernet structure and cassis cut the fat beautifully.
The wine's power and structure are matched by the richness of a marrow-enriched steak.
In older vintages, Pichon Baron develops earthy, mushroom tones that echo the filling.

history.summary: The Pichon estate was divided in 1850 between Baron and Comtesse heirs; both halves were classified as Second Growths in 1855. The Baron half, now Pichon-Longueville Baron, was acquired by AXA Millesimes in 1987 along with major cellar renovations. Alexandra Lebossé has been winemaker since 2017.

milestones.event: Classified as Deuxieme Cru Classe in the Medoc classification
Acquired by AXA Millesimes; major winery reconstruction and quality improvement begins
Wine Advocate 98-point vintage (William Kelley in-bottle review, March 2025)

wines.description: Pauillac second growth with an unusually Cabernet-dominant blend for the appellation. AXA Millesimes-owned since 1987; the 2022 vintage drew especially strong critical praise in bottle.

wines.name: Chateau Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande
wines.producer_name: Chateau Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande
wines.classification: Pauillac AOC, Deuxieme Cru Classe (1855 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 50% new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Known for its silky texture and unusually high 32% Merlot for a Left Bank estate, Pichon Lalande bridges the power of Pauillac with a suppleness and perfume reminiscent of Margaux. Louis Roederer ownership since 2006 has brought further precision.

pairings.dish: Roast pigeon with black truffle sauce
pairings.why: The wine's silky texture and tobacco-truffle notes mirror the delicacy and earthiness of roasted pigeon.

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with rosemary jus
pairings.why: Classic Pauillac pairing; Pichon Lalande's softer tannins and cassis make it especially suited to the delicacy of spring rack.
Hard, crystalline French cheese amplifies the cassis and pencil-shaving aromatic register.

history.summary: The Pichon estate was divided in 1850 between Baron and Comtesse heirs. The Comtesse half, facing the Chateau Latour vineyards from across the road, was purchased by the Miailhe family and rose to international fame under May-Eliane de Lencquesaing's direction from 1978 to 2006. Louis Roederer Champagne acquired it in 2006.

milestones.event: Classified as Deuxieme Cru Classe in the Medoc classification
May-Eliane de Lencquesaing takes control; begins elevation of estate reputation
Acquired by Louis Roederer Champagne
Wine Advocate 100 points for the 2022 vintage (William Kelley, March 2025 in-bottle review)

wines.description: Pauillac second growth with an unusually high Merlot proportion (32%) for the Left Bank, giving silky texture and floral perfume. Louis Roederer-owned since 2006; 2022 earned Wine Advocate's perfect score from William Kelley.

wines.name: Reserve de la Comtesse
wines.producer_name: Chateau Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 50% new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Second wine of Pichon Lalande since 1973. Described by the estate as a true Pauillac brand in its own right. Higher Petit Verdot proportion adds structure and spice; the estate's characteristic silk texture is detectable even at this level.

pairings.dish: Roast chicken with thyme and garlic
pairings.why: The second wine's accessible tannins and fruit-forward palate pair beautifully with roasted poultry's natural sweetness.
The cassis and tobacco notes echo the slow-braised red wine flavours in the stew; medium body matches the textures.

pairings.dish: Roasted duck breast with cherry compote
pairings.why: Dark fruit in the wine bridges the cherry reduction; duck fat is managed by firm Cabernet structure.

history.summary: Reserve de la Comtesse has been produced since 1973, making it one of the earlier established second wines in the Medoc. Under Louis Roederer ownership it has been further developed as a standalone wine rather than just an overflow of the grand vin selection process.

milestones.event: First vintage of Reserve de la Comtesse; among the earliest formal second wines in the Medoc

wines.description: Second wine of Pichon Lalande since 1973, one of the earlier established Medoc second wines. The estate's characteristic silk texture and Pauillac cassis profile at an accessible price.

wines.name: Les Hauts de Pontet-Canet
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in 30% new French oak barriques and neutral vessels

taste.summary: Second wine of Pontet-Canet since 1982. Produced in limited quantities as Alfred Tesseron prefers lower yields and declassification over large second-wine production; earlier-drinking, approachable Pauillac from biodynamic-certified vineyards.

pairings.why: The biodynamic fruit purity of Les Hauts de Pontet-Canet shines with simply grilled lamb; no heavy sauce needed.
Accessible tannins and fresh fruit handle preserved duck charcuterie without overpowering delicate preservation.

pairings.dish: Roast chicken thighs with root vegetables
pairings.why: Medium body and approachable structure make this an ideal everyday pairing for roasted poultry.

history.summary: First produced in 1982. Production is intentionally modest because Tesseron family prefers lower grand vin yields over large second-wine declassification volumes. In several recent vintages the wine has not been produced at all. The estate's biodynamic certification extends to all vines.

milestones.event: First vintage of Les Hauts de Pontet-Canet

wines.description: Second wine of Pontet-Canet since 1982, produced in intentionally limited quantities. From the same Demeter-certified biodynamic vineyards as the grand vin; more approachable and earlier-drinking.

wines.name: Chateau Pontet-Canet
wines.classification: Pauillac AOC, Cinquieme Cru Classe (1855 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 14 to 16 months in 50% new barrels, 35% amphora, 15% once-used wood

taste.finish: extraordinary length; tannins of incredible precision
taste.summary: The leading biodynamic estate in Bordeaux and the only Fifth Growth that regularly receives 100-point scores. Demeter-certified since 2014; unique use of amphorae alongside barrique ageing. The wines combine Pauillac's classical structure with a biodynamic freshness and earth energy not found elsewhere in the Medoc.

pairings.dish: Roasted rack of lamb with black olive tapenade
pairings.why: Pontet-Canet's biodynamic precision and cassis intensity pair with the mineral and savoury tapenade; lamb's sweetness anchors the frame.

pairings.dish: Braised beef short rib with bone marrow gremolata
pairings.why: The wine's powerful tannin architecture and dark fruit intensity need the richness of slow-braised beef.

pairings.dish: Wild boar with juniper and rosehip
pairings.why: Biodynamic Pontet-Canet's earthy complexity, mineral core, and dark fruit are designed for game; juniper amplifies the wine's wild herb register.
In outstanding vintages, the wine develops truffle and forest floor notes that connect with truffle-based dishes elegantly.

history.summary: The Tesseron family acquired Pontet-Canet in 1975. Alfred Tesseron began the conversion to biodynamics in 2004; the estate received Biodyvin certification in 2010 and full Demeter certification in 2014 -- the first major Medoc Cru Classe to do so. The 2010 and 2012 vintages both earned perfect 100-point scores from Wine Advocate, establishing Pontet-Canet as one of the most exciting estates in Bordeaux.

milestones.event: Acquired by the Tesseron family
Biodynamic conversion begins under Alfred Tesseron
Wine Advocate 100 points; biodynamic approach vindicated at the highest level
Second consecutive Wine Advocate 100-point vintage; Demeter certification achieved two years later
Full Demeter biodynamic certification; first major Medoc Cru Classe to achieve this

wines.description: Bordeaux's leading biodynamic estate; first major Medoc Cru Classe with Demeter certification (2014). Fifth Growth that regularly earns 100-point scores; unique amphora-and-barrique ageing regime.

wines.name: Les Carmes de Rieussec
wines.oak_regime: French oak barrels; 18 months

taste.summary: The second sweet wine of Chateau Rieussec, named after the Carmelite monks who founded the estate. Shares the same high-Semillon blend and botrytised character as the grand vin, with apricot, candied ginger, saffron, and orange blossom in a sweeter, more approachable style.

pairings.why: Classic Sauternes pairing; the wine's sweetness and acidity cut through the luxurious richness of foie gras terrine.
Stone fruit in the dessert mirrors the wine's apricot and tangerine notes; cream bridges the two.
Salty blue cheese and dried fruit create resonance with the wine's apricot compote and candied-ginger character.

history.summary: Les Carmes de Rieussec was introduced in 1975 as the second Sauternes wine of the estate, named in honour of the Carmelite monks who originally cultivated the Rieussec vineyard. It is produced from younger vines and lots not selected for the grand vin, aged in the same oak regime.

milestones.event: Les Carmes de Rieussec introduced as the second sweet wine of the estate

wines.description: Second Sauternes wine of Chateau Rieussec, named after the Carmelite monks who founded the estate. Semillon-dominant botrytised wine with apricot, candied ginger, saffron, and orange blossom; sweet and approachable with a long finish.

wines.name: Chateau Rieussec
wines.oak_regime: 50% new French oak; 18 to 24 months

taste.summary: One of the most consistent Premier Cru Classe Sauternes, with Rieussec's distinctive high Semillon content yielding a wine of great concentration and rich tropical fruit. Honey-drenched pineapple, orange, mango, and caramel with vibrant acidity and a very long finish.

pairings.why: The classic Sauternes foie gras pairing; the wine's sweetness and acidity balance the unctuous richness of mi-cuit foie.
Salty, pungent blue cheese is tamed by the wine's honey and tropical fruit; acidity lifts the pairing.
Caramel-on-caramel richness; the wine's high acidity prevents the combination from cloying.
Stone fruit in both wine and dessert creates a harmonious resonance; caramelised pastry echoes Sauternes complexity.
Fruit and blue cheese pairing; sweetness and acidity balance pungent cheese while pear echoes the wine's nectarine notes.

history.summary: Chateau Rieussec is the largest Premier Cru Classe Sauternes estate at approximately 93 hectares, located in the communes of Fargues-de-Langon and Sauternes bordering Chateau d'Yquem. Originally founded by Carmelite monks, the property was confiscated during the French Revolution. Acquired by Albert Vuillier in 1971, it was subsequently purchased by Domaines Barons de Rothschild (Lafite) in 1984, which has managed it to premier-growth standards since.

milestones.event: Classified Premier Cru Classe in the Sauternes-Barsac classification
Carmes de Rieussec second wine introduced
Domaines Barons de Rothschild (Lafite) acquires Chateau Rieussec
R de Rieussec dry white introduced

wines.description: Largest Premier Cru Classe Sauternes estate at 93 hectares, owned by Domaines Barons de Rothschild (Lafite) since 1984. Semillon-dominant botrytised wine; honey-drenched pineapple, orange, and mango with caramel complexity and very long finish.

wines.name: R de Rieussec
wines.oak_regime: Approximately 20% new French oak; 12 months

taste.summary: The dry white wine of Chateau Rieussec, a relative newcomer first produced in 1999. A fresh, citrussy Sauvignon Blanc and Semillon blend from the Fargues-de-Langon terroir with herbal and mineral freshness; lighter-styled than the Sauternes grand vin but sharing its Semillon richness.

pairings.why: High-acid citrus drive is the classic oyster companion; the Semillon adds a subtle richness.
Sweet crab and creamy avocado find a refreshing counterbalance in the wine's lime acidity and chalk mineral.
Herbal and slightly bitter asparagus echo the Sauvignon's freshness; vinaigrette's acidity aligns with the wine.

history.summary: R de Rieussec was introduced in 1999 as a dry white Bordeaux from the same Fargues-de-Langon terroir as the Sauternes grand vin. It uses the classification Bordeaux AOC rather than Sauternes (which is reserved for sweet wines) and is intended to demonstrate the terroir's versatility for dry white production.

milestones.event: R de Rieussec introduced as a dry white Bordeaux from the Sauternes estate

wines.description: Dry white wine from Chateau Rieussec, introduced in 1999. Sauvignon Blanc-Semillon blend from the Fargues-de-Langon gravel terroir under Bordeaux AOC; fresh, citrus-driven and mineral with crisp acidity.

wines.name: Chateau Roc de Cambes
wines.oak_regime: 18-24 months in 50% new French oak barrels; entirely new oak since 1996

taste.summary: Francois Mitjavile's Cotes de Bourg cult wine, modelled on the exacting principles of his Saint-Emilion estate Tertre-Roteboeuf: very late harvesting, low yields, and 100% new oak since 1996. Pushed the Cotes de Bourg to its quality ceiling.

pairings.dish: Magret de canard with pepper sauce
pairings.why: Rich duck fat and the wine's cassis and spice complement each other; peppercorn echoes the dried herb character.
Full body and firm tannins cut through marrow richness; limestone minerality lifts the finish.
Earthy mushroom and washed-rind cheese find a partner in the wine's smoked meat and cigar wrapper register.
A great match for Merlot-led Right Bank reds; gelatinous slow-cooked fat is tamed by the wine's tannic structure.

history.summary: Francois Mitjavile acquired the Roc de Cambes estate in 1987-1988 from bankruptcy, renaming it Domaine Roc de Cambes and applying the same painstaking approach he uses at Tertre-Roteboeuf in Saint-Emilion. The Malbec and Cabernet Franc planted at acquisition have since been replaced entirely by Merlot and Cabernet Sauvignon. From 1996 the wine has been aged in entirely new oak.

milestones.event: First vintage produced under Francois Mitjavile's ownership
100% new oak regime adopted, aligned with Tertre-Roteboeuf approach
Wine Advocate awards 95 points, establishing Roc de Cambes as cult Cotes de Bourg

wines.description: Cotes de Bourg cult wine from Francois Mitjavile of Tertre-Roteboeuf; 80% Merlot, 20% Cabernet Sauvignon on limestone slopes. 100% new oak since 1996; concentrated, structured, and generous. First vintage 1988.

wines.name: Le Petit Haut Lafitte Blanc
wines.oak_regime: 50% new French oak; 12 months

taste.summary: The second white wine of Smith Haut Lafitte. Sauvignon Blanc-driven with Semillon adding roundness; lime tree, verbena, and citrus oil aromas lead a fresh, floral palate with elderflower and white peach. Vibrant acidity makes it an excellent aperitif and shellfish partner.

pairings.why: Racy Sauvignon Blanc acidity is the traditional oyster partner; citrus and mineral notes amplify brine.

pairings.dish: Grilled sole with capers and lemon
pairings.why: Crisp acidity and citrus fruit complement delicate white fish; capers echo the wine's herbal character.
High acidity cuts through buttery hollandaise; lime and herbal freshness aligns with asparagus.

history.summary: Created alongside the second red wine in the early Cathiard era as the affordable counterpart to Smith Haut Lafitte Blanc. The white is unusually high in Sauvignon Blanc content, matching the philosophy of the grand vin blanc.

milestones.event: Le Petit Haut Lafitte Blanc introduced as the second white wine of the estate

wines.description: Second white wine of Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte. Sauvignon Blanc-dominant with Semillon; lime tree, verbena, and citrus oil lead a fresh, mineral palate ideal for aperitif and shellfish.

verified.address_quoted: Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte, 33650 Martillac, France

wines.name: Le Petit Haut Lafitte Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Approximately 20 to 30% new French oak; 14 months

taste.summary: The second red wine of Smith Haut Lafitte, sharing the estate's Martillac gravel terroir. A Cabernet-Sauvignon-led blend with cassis, fresh herbs, and pencil-shaving notes in an approachable medium-bodied style suitable for earlier drinking.

pairings.why: The wine's medium body and cassis fruit are a natural counterpoint to rich pork; mustard amplifies the herbal freshness.
Soft tannin and dark fruit complement a beef-rich sauce; approachable style suits a casual dinner.
Medium body balances the richness of cream; herbal notes echo tarragon.

history.summary: Le Petit Haut Lafitte was created in the early years of the Cathiard era as the estate's second red wine, aimed at providing the character of Martillac gravel Cabernet at a more accessible price point. It shares the same vineyard and cellar infrastructure as the grand vin.

milestones.event: Le Petit Haut Lafitte Rouge introduced as the second red wine of Smith Haut Lafitte

wines.description: Second red wine of Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte. Cabernet-Sauvignon-dominant blend from Martillac; cassis, herbs, and pencil-shaving notes in a fresh, approachable medium-bodied style.

verified.address_quoted: Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte, 33650 Martillac, France

wines.name: Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte Blanc
wines.classification: Cru Classe de Graves (white); Pessac-Leognan AOC
wines.oak_regime: 45% new French oak; 12 months; partial barrel fermentation

taste.summary: A consistently top-rated Pessac-Leognan white dominated by Sauvignon Blanc with distinctive Sauvignon Gris character. Honeyed grapefruit, crushed stone, and elderflower on the nose; vibrant citrus and mineral freshness on the palate. Frequently earns 95-98 points in recent vintages.

pairings.why: The wine's mineral-saline drive and citrus precision align with the briny sweetness of Atlantic oysters.
Sweet crustacean and herbed butter find a partner in the wine's elderflower and citrus freshness.
Saffron echoes the wine's stone-fruit register while sweet scallop complements its mineral depth.

pairings.dish: Chevre frais with lemon and herbs
pairings.why: Classic Sauvignon Blanc and goat's cheese pairing; the wine's citrus acidity amplifies the fresh cheese tang.

history.summary: The white wine of Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte came into prominence alongside the red after the Cathiard acquisition in 1990. Dominated by Sauvignon Blanc -- unusual for Pessac-Leognan whites, which more typically lean toward Semillon -- the wine has become one of the appellation's most celebrated whites, earning scores of 96-98 points in recent vintages.

milestones.event: Cathiard family begin modern era; white wine quality elevated alongside red
Smith Haut Lafitte Blanc establishes itself among the top Pessac-Leognan whites

wines.description: Sauvignon Blanc-dominant Cru Classe de Graves white consistently earning 95-98 points. Honeyed grapefruit, crushed stone, and elderflower lead a mineral, long-finishing palate. Among the finest whites in Pessac-Leognan.

verified.address_quoted: Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte, 33650 Martillac, France

wines.name: Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 60% new French oak; 16 to 18 months

taste.summary: A benchmark Pessac-Leognan Cabernet since the Cathiard family's acquisition in 1990. Cassis, graphite, and tobacco define a precise, structured palate; 60% new oak ageing adds cedar and spice. Consistent 97-98 point scores in recent vintages.

pairings.dish: Roasted Pauillac lamb with garlic and herbs
pairings.why: Cabernet structure and herbal notes are the classic match for roasted lamb; cassis depth echoes the reduction.

pairings.dish: Grilled sirloin with green peppercorn sauce
pairings.why: Firm tannins cut through beef fat; the wine's graphite and spice amplify the peppercorn savour.
Dark berry fruit complements duck richness; the compote mirrors the wine's cassis and dark-plum character.
Truffle earthiness and layered dark fruit in the wine align with the earthy complexity of a wellington.

history.summary: Although Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte dates to the 18th century, the modern era of quality wine begins with Daniel and Florence Cathiard's acquisition in 1990. Their investment in viticulture and winemaking equipment transformed the estate into one of Pessac-Leognan's most consistent performers, frequently scoring in the high 90s. The Cathiards also built Les Sources de Caudalie, a wine spa resort on the estate.

milestones.event: George Smith, a Scottish tea merchant, acquires the estate and names it Smith Haut Lafitte
Classified as Cru Classe de Graves in the Graves classification
Daniel and Florence Cathiard purchase the estate; quality transformation begins
Les Sources de Caudalie wine spa resort opens on the estate

wines.description: Benchmark Pessac-Leognan Cru Classe red led by Cabernet Sauvignon since the Cathiard family transformed the estate from 1990. Cassis, graphite, tobacco, and 60% new-oak structure; outstanding ageing potential.

verified.address_quoted: Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte, 33650 Martillac, France

wines.name: Château Sociando-Mallet
wines.oak_regime: 95% new French oak barrels, 12 months

taste.summary: The great unclassified Médoc: Sociando-Mallet produces wines of classed-growth quality from Saint-Seurin-de-Cadourne, with muscular Cabernet-Merlot structure, minty cassis, and a distinctive saline minerality.

pairings.dish: Côte de boeuf with Bordelaise sauce
pairings.why: The wine's powerful structure and dark cassis fruit are made for a bone-in rib roast with classic Bordelaise.
Gamey, spiced venison meets the wine's muscular dark fruit and mineral depth in a compelling match.
Earthy, firm mountain cheese integrates the wine's tannins and amplifies its savory mineral notes.
Long roasting creates a richness and tenderness that requires Sociando's structural power to balance.

history.summary: Jean Gautreau purchased a neglected Haut-Médoc property in 1969 and transformed it into one of Bordeaux's most respected unclassified estates. His obsessive quality standards - 95% new oak and strict selection - produced wines that consistently rival classed growths. In 1995 a special Cuvée Jean Gautreau was produced from the 80 best barrels. The estate passed to new ownership after Gautreau's retirement.

milestones.event: Jean Gautreau acquires the neglected estate and begins quality revival
Cuvée Jean Gautreau produced from barrel selection as a special one-off
Final vintage fully under Jean Gautreau's direction

wines.description: The great unclassified Haut-Médoc: built by Jean Gautreau from 1969 into a wine that regularly outperforms classed growths. Muscular, age-worthy, and one of Bordeaux's best hidden gems for those who know.

verified.address_quoted: CHATEAU SOCIANDO-MALLET ROUTE DE MAPON 33180 SAINT-SEURIN-DE-CADOURNE

wines.name: La Demoiselle de Sociando-Mallet
wines.oak_regime: 25% new French oak barrels and concrete vats, 10-12 months

taste.summary: The Merlot-dominant second wine of Sociando-Mallet, offering earlier approachability with a more typically Médoc fruit profile than the austere and structured grand vin.

pairings.why: The wine's approachable dark fruit and medium grip are a classic match for a simple steak dinner.
Merlot richness and red fruit complement duck's natural affinity for berry flavors.
The wine's supple tannins and cassis notes suit the mineral sweetness of grilled lamb.

history.summary: La Demoiselle de Sociando-Mallet is the second wine of Château Sociando-Mallet, introduced around 1985 (formerly known as Lartigue-de Brochon). With a higher Merlot content than the grand vin, it is more accessible in youth and a fraction of the price.

milestones.event: Second wine introduced under the Lartigue-de Brochon label
Renamed La Demoiselle de Sociando-Mallet

wines.description: Second wine of Château Sociando-Mallet, Merlot-dominant for earlier approachability than the powerful grand vin. Excellent value Haut-Médoc from one of Bordeaux's greatest unclassified estates.

verified.address_quoted: CHATEAU SOCIANDO-MALLET ROUTE DE MAPON 33180 SAINT-SEURIN-DE-CADOURNE

wines.name: Chateau Troplong Mondot
wines.classification: Saint-Emilion Premier Grand Cru Classe B (2022 classification)
wines.oak_regime: 16-24 months in 50-65% new French oak barrels; recent vintages include a portion in large foudres

taste.finish: long, concentrated with floral and chocolate notes
taste.summary: From Saint-Emilion's highest plateau (over 100m), Troplong Mondot produces Merlot-dominant blends of great richness and depth. Thick and concentrated without being heavy; dark plum, coffee and stone character with remarkable ageing potential.

pairings.why: Full-body and concentration match the richness of prime entrecote; blue cheese butter mirrors the wine's dark mineral complexity.

pairings.dish: Braised short rib with root vegetables
pairings.why: Slow-cooked collagen and sweet root vegetable sweetness integrate with dense Merlot fruit and firm tannins.

pairings.dish: Wild boar stew with juniper berries
pairings.why: Gamey wild boar finds harmony with the wine's dark-fruit intensity and earthy spice.
High-cocoa chocolate amplifies the wine's mocha and dark-fruit depth in an indulgent dessert pairing.

history.summary: Chateau Troplong Mondot, established on Saint-Emilion's highest limestone plateau, experienced a quality renaissance under Christine Valette from the late 1980s, particularly from the acclaimed 1989 vintage. Promoted to Premier Grand Cru Classe B in 2006, the estate was acquired by French insurance company SCOR in 2017 for a record 7 million euros per hectare. A major cellar renovation was completed in 2020. The estate now includes a Michelin-starred restaurant, Les Belles Perdrix.

milestones.event: First acclaimed modern vintage under Christine Valette's direction
Promoted to Premier Grand Cru Classe B in the revised Saint-Emilion classification
Acquired by SCOR insurance group for a record 7 million euros per hectare
Major cellar renovation completed

wines.description: Grand vin from Chateau Troplong Mondot, Saint-Emilion Premier Grand Cru Classe B. Merlot-dominant blend from the appellation's highest limestone plateau, producing rich, concentrated wines with dark plum, coffee and mineral depth.

wines.name: Mondot
wines.oak_regime: Aged in a mix of foudres and used oak barrels; from 2024 vintage majority in foudres for freshness

taste.summary: Second wine of Troplong Mondot, Merlot-dominant with emphasis on freshness and early drinkability. A juicy, earthy black cherry and spring flower profile with wet-stone limestone terroir character; approachable and unpretentious.

pairings.why: Juicy Merlot fruit and fresh acidity cut through the fat and spice of grilled charcuterie.
Medium-body and red-fruit character align with tomato-based meat sauce; light mineral finish lifts the dish.
Polished tannins integrate with beef patty richness; the wine's fruit freshness cuts through the cheese.

history.summary: Mondot was created in 1985 as the second wine of Chateau Troplong Mondot. Drawing on younger and less concentrated parcel selections from the same limestone plateau vineyard, it offers everyday access to the estate's distinctive terroir character. The wine has evolved with the estate's broader renovation from 2017-2020, adopting a fresher, foudre-aged style in recent years.

milestones.event: First vintage of Mondot produced as second wine of Troplong Mondot

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Troplong Mondot, first produced in 1985. Merlot-dominant with juicy red fruit and wet-stone limestone character; approachable and fresh, designed for earlier drinking.

wines.name: Le Pin
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 100% new French oak barrels; malolactic fermentation in barrel

taste.summary: Micro-estate garagiste Pomerol with supremely hedonistic Merlot character: mocha-laced plum and violet aromatics, a voluptuous, fleshy palate, and remarkable concentration from just 400-600 cases per year.

pairings.dish: Roast lamb with rosemary and garlic
pairings.why: The plush Merlot fruit and vanilla oak frame the sweet lamb fat beautifully; herbal rosemary echoes cedar notes.
Sweet dried fruit and dark cherry in the wine are a natural counterpart to rich poultry and fruity accompaniments.
The earthy luxury of truffle and richness of butter are matched by the wine's mocha depth and full body.
Nutty crystalline cheese amplifies the wine's cedar and spice while the tannins stay integrated.

history.summary: Le Pin was purchased from bankruptcy in 1979 by Jacques Thienpont (cousin of the Thienpont family at Vieux Chateau Certan) for a modest sum. Robert Parker's enthusiastic praise of the 1982 vintage transformed it into one of the most sought-after and expensive wines in the world. The 2003 entire vintage was declassified by the estate. Jacques's son Axel Thienpont manages the property today.

milestones.event: First vintage produced by Jacques Thienpont
Parker acclaim launches Le Pin's cult status
Entire vintage declassified due to extreme heat

wines.description: Ultra-micro 2.7-hectare Pomerol estate producing 400-600 cases of 100% Merlot annually. One of the most expensive wines in Bordeaux; first vintage 1979; Jacques Thienpont's garagiste creation.

wines.name: Petrus
wines.oak_regime: 18-20 months in 50% new French oak barrels

taste.summary: 100% Merlot from 11.4 ha of blue clay at Pomerol's centre. Silk-and-velvet texture with truffle, chocolate, and dark cherry at its core; structurally imposing yet hedonistic, with a finish that stretches for minutes.

pairings.why: The wine's truffle register mirrors the earthy garnish; ripe Merlot tannins cut cleanly through the butter sauce.
Crystalline nutty cheeses amplify the chocolate and spice notes without fighting the wine's weight.

pairings.dish: Roasted Bresse pigeon with black cherry jus
pairings.why: The dark-fruit palate and gentle iron minerality complement the gamey sweetness of the bird.
Earthy porcini and truffle echo the wine's secondary aromatics; the creaminess softens tannin.

history.summary: Records of Petrus date to the mid-1750s, but the estate rose to international fame after Mme. Edmond Loubat became sole owner in 1945 and promoted it at top Paris restaurants. Jean-Pierre Moueix acquired a half-share in 1969; the Moueix family retains control today under Jean-Francois Moueix. Olivier Berrouet succeeded his father Jean-Claude as winemaker in 2008. The entire Cabernet Franc was progressively replaced with Merlot from the late 2000s.

milestones.event: Gold medal at the Paris Exposition Universelle, first Pomerol wine to command Medoc second-growth prices
Mme. Loubat becomes sole owner; the modern era of Petrus begins
Jean-Pierre Moueix acquires a half-share; distribution through Etablissements Jean-Pierre Moueix, Libourne
Olivier Berrouet takes over from his father Jean-Claude as head winemaker

wines.description: The singular 100% Merlot Pomerol from 11.4 ha of blue clay. No second wine; all rejected lots sold as generic Pomerol. Olivier Berrouet winemaker; production around 30,000 bottles per year.

wines.name: La Gravette de Certan
wines.oak_regime: 15-18 months in French oak, lower proportion new oak than grand vin

taste.summary: The accessible, Merlot-dominant second selection from Vieux Chateau Certan, made from younger vines and less-selected barrels. Forward, fruit-driven, and approachable early but retaining the estate's hallmark iron-mineral signature.

pairings.why: Soft tannins and fruit-forward Merlot complement the mild richness of chicken; mushroom sauce echoes earthy notes.
Medium body and soft tannins work with young lamb; leather and tobacco add complexity.
The wine's plummy character and acidity cut through the slow-braised richness of the dish.

history.summary: La Gravette de Certan was created in 1986 by Alexandre Thienpont as the second wine of Vieux Chateau Certan, drawing its name from a parcel of vines purchased by the family in 1946. In recent vintages it has been produced entirely from 100% Merlot from younger vines, offering an accessible entry to the VCC terroir.

milestones.event: First vintage of La Gravette de Certan produced by Alexandre Thienpont
Vintage scored 93 points from Vinous

wines.description: Second wine of Vieux Chateau Certan, first produced in 1986. 100% Merlot from younger vines; forward, fruit-driven, and approachable with the Pomerol iron-mineral signature at a fraction of the grand vin price.

wines.name: Vieux Chateau Certan
wines.oak_regime: 20 months in approximately 50% new French oak; fined with egg whites, unfiltered

taste.summary: Pomerol's most Cabernet-Franc-inflected grand vin: 30% Cabernet Franc gives an aromatic lift and structural precision that sets VCC apart from Merlot-dominant peers. Sensuous and silky in great vintages, with decades of ageing potential.

pairings.dish: Roasted rack of lamb with herbs
pairings.why: Cabernet Franc lifts bring an herbal edge that mirrors the aromatics of rosemary and thyme; the wine's structure handles the fat.
The wine's truffle and dark-earth aromas harmonise with mushroom intensity; plush body coats rich sauces.
Iron minerality and dark berry flavours are a classic pairing with the gamy sweetness of young pigeon.
Caramelised nut notes in mature cheese amplify the chocolate and blue-fruit finish.

history.summary: The Thienpont family acquired Vieux Chateau Certan in 1924, making it one of the longest family-owned estates in Pomerol. The 14-hectare contiguous vineyard was planted by the previous owner and retains unusually high proportions of Cabernet Franc for the appellation. The 2024 vintage marked the centenary of Thienpont ownership; Guillaume Thienpont currently manages and vinifies the estate.

milestones.event: Thienpont family acquires the estate
La Gravette de Certan second wine launched
First 100-point Wine Advocate score for VCC
Centenary of Thienpont ownership celebrated with vertical tasting

wines.description: Thienpont family-owned since 1924, Pomerol's most Cabernet-Franc-forward grand vin. 14 contiguous hectares; 65% Merlot, 30% Cabernet Franc, 5% Cabernet Sauvignon; egg-white fined, unfiltered.
